# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the properties of a rotation matrix.**

**Model Answer:**

A rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ satisfies the following properties:

1. **Orthogonality:** $R^T R = R R^T = I$ — preserves lengths and angles
2. **Unit Determinant:** $\det(R) = +1$ — preserves right-handed coordinate frame
3. **Inverse Property:** $R^{-1} = R^T$ — inversion is computationally trivial
4. **Identity Element:** $R I = I R = R$ — no rotation corresponds to identity
5. **Closure:** $R_1 R_2 \in SO(3)$ — product of two rotation matrices is also a valid rotation matrix

> [!NOTE]
> These 5 properties are the **exact set** expected in KTU 3-mark short-answer questions.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Define a free vector. How is it different from a position vector?**

**Model Answer:**

**Free Vector:** A vector fully described by its **magnitude and direction** alone, with no fixed point of application. It can be translated anywhere in space without changing its physical meaning. Examples: force, velocity, angular velocity.

**Position Vector (Line Vector):** A vector that has a **specific point of application** (or origin reference). Its location in space matters. Example: the position of a point P relative to the origin O is denoted $^OP$.

| Feature | Free Vector | Position Vector |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| Defined by | Magnitude + Direction | Magnitude + Direction + Reference Point |
| Translation invariance | Yes | No |
| Example | Angular velocity $\omega$ | Position of end-effector $^BP_E$ |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
A frame $\{B\}$ is obtained by rotating frame $\{A\}$ by $30°$ about the Z-axis, followed by a rotation of $45°$ about the Y-axis. Find the rotation matrix $^A_BR$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A point P has coordinates $^BP = [2, 3, 4]^T$ in frame $\{B\}$. Find its representation in frame $\{A\}$ using the rotation matrix obtained in part (a).

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the elementary rotations:
- $R_Z(30°)$: Rotation about Z by $30°$
- $R_Y(45°)$: Rotation about Y by $45°$

**Step 2:** Compute the trigonometric values:
- $\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$
- $\cos 45° = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.7071$, $\sin 45° = 0.7071$

**Step 3:** Write the elementary rotation matrices:

$$R_Z(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.8660 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_Y(45°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the post-multiplication rule (the rotation performed **last** appears on the **right**):

$$^A_BR = R_Z(30°) \cdot R_Y(45°)$$

**Step 5:** Perform matrix multiplication element by element. Row 1, Col 1:

$$(0.8660)(0.7071) + (-0.5)(0) + (0)(-0.7071) = 0.6124$$

Row 1, Col 2:

$$(0.8660)(0) + (-0.5)(1) + (0)(0) = -0.5$$

Row 1, Col 3:

$$(0.8660)(0.7071) + (-0.5)(0) + (0)(0.7071) = 0.6124$$

Row 2, Col 1:

$$(0.5)(0.7071) + (0.8660)(0) + (0)(-0.7071) = 0.3536$$

Row 2, Col 2:

$$(0.5)(0) + (0.8660)(1) + (0)(0) = 0.8660$$

Row 2, Col 3:

$$(0.5)(0.7071) + (0.8660)(0) + (0)(0.7071) = 0.3536$$

Row 3, Col 1:

$$(0)(0.7071) + (0)(0) + (1)(-0.7071) = -0.7071$$

Row 3, Col 2:

$$(0)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) = 0$$

Row 3, Col 3:

$$(0)(0.7071) + (0)(0) + (1)(0.7071) = 0.7071$$

**Step 6:** Final rotation matrix:

$$^A_BR = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6124 & -0.5 & 0.6124 \\ 0.3536 & 0.8660 & 0.3536 \\ -0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** $\det(^A_BR) = 0.6124(0.8660 \times 0.7071 - 0.3536 \times 0) - (-0.5)(0.3536 \times 0.7071 - 0.3536 \times (-0.7071)) + 0.6124(0.3536 \times 0 - 0.8660 \times (-0.7071)) = 0.6124 \times 0.6124 + 0.5 \times (0.250 + 0.250) + 0.6124 \times 0.6124 \approx 0.375 + 0.250 + 0.375 = 1.0$ ✓

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the transformation $^AP = \,^A_BR \cdot \,^BP$:

$$^AP = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6124 & -0.5 & 0.6124 \\ 0.3536 & 0.8660 & 0.3536 \\ -0.7071 & 0 & 0.7071 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute each row:

- $x$-component: $(0.6124)(2) + (-0.5)(3) + (0.6124)(4) = 1.2248 - 1.5 + 2.4496 = 2.1744$
- $y$-component: $(0.3536)(2) + (0.8660)(3) + (0.3536)(4) = 0.7072 + 2.598 + 1.4144 = 4.7196$
- $z$-component: $(-0.7071)(2) + (0)(3) + (0.7071)(4) = -1.4142 + 0 + 2.8284 = 1.4142$

**Step 3:** Final answer:

$$^AP = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1744 \\ 4.7196 \\ 1.4142 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Derive the elementary rotation matrix for a rotation by angle $\theta$ about the X-axis. State clearly the right-hand rule convention.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Show that two rotations about the same axis commute, while two rotations about different axes do not. Provide one numerical counter-example for the non-commutative case.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:** (See Section 3.2 for analogous Z-derivation; for X-axis):

**Step 1:** Consider a vector in the YZ-plane rotated about the X-axis by angle $\theta$.

**Step 2:** Apply the right-hand rule: thumb along +X, fingers curl from +Y toward +Z. This gives the sign convention.

**Step 3:** The transformation equations are:

$$y' = y\cos\theta - z\sin\theta$$

$$z' = y\sin\theta + z\cos\theta$$

$$x' = x$$

**Step 4:** Matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$R_X(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

> ✓ Note the structural pattern: the 1 is on the diagonal at the rotation axis, and the $\cos\theta$ entries form a 2×2 sub-block.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Same axis case (commutative):**
We showed in Section 3.4 that $R_Z(\alpha) R_Z(\beta) = R_Z(\alpha + \beta) = R_Z(\beta + \alpha) = R_Z(\beta) R_Z(\alpha)$. Thus rotations about the same axis commute.

**Different axis case (non-commutative) — Numerical counter-example:**

Take $\alpha = 90°$ about X-axis, $\beta = 90°$ about Y-axis.

**Step 1:** Compute $R_X(90°)$ and $R_Y(90°)$:

$$R_X(90°) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_Y(90°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $R_X(90°) R_Y(90°)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $R_Y(90°) R_X(90°)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compare the two results:

$$R_X(90°) R_Y(90°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \neq \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = R_Y(90°) R_X(90°)$$

> ✓ **Conclusion:** Rotations about different axes are **non-commutative** in 3D space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** (Loss: 2-3 marks): Students often forget the right-hand rule and write $+\sin\theta$ instead of $-\sin\theta$ in $R_X$ and $R_Z$ matrices. Always re-derive carefully.
> 2. **Pre/Post-multiplication Confusion** (Loss: 2 marks): When composing rotations, the matrix corresponding to the **first** rotation goes on the **left**, not the right.
> 3. **Confusing Rotation vs. Representation** (Loss: 3 marks): $R^B_A$ rotates a vector's *frame* vs. rotates a vector's *components*. KTU expects students to clearly distinguish these two interpretations.
> 4. **Forgetting to Verify Orthogonality** (Loss: 1 mark): In 14-mark problems, always conclude by checking that $R R^T = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$ to earn full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Fixed and Euler Angle notation** (Loss: 2 marks): X-Y-Z fixed angles and Z-Y-Z Euler angles are different — read the question carefully.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ A **free vector** is defined only by magnitude and direction — it is invariant under pure translation.
- ☐ A **position vector** is anchored to a reference point; it is *not* a free vector.
- ☐ **Translation** changes position only; **Rotation** changes orientation only.
- ☐ A rigid body in 3D has **6 DOF**: 3 translational + 3 rotational.
- ☐ A **rotation matrix** is a $3 \times 3$ orthogonal matrix with $\det(R) = +1$, i.e., $R \in SO(3)$.
- ☐ Key properties: $R^T R = I$, $R^{-1} = R^T$, $\det(R) = +1$.
- ☐ **Elementary rotation matrices**: $R_X$, $R_Y$, $R_Z$ — each is a 2D rotation in a plane perpendicular to the axis.
- ☐ **Right-Hand Rule** is the standard sign convention in KTU robotics problems.
- ☐ The **operator interpretation**: $^AP = \,^A_BR \cdot \,^BP$ — same physical vector, different coordinate components.
- ☐ Rotation composition: $^A_CR = \,^A_BR \cdot \,^B_CR$ — **post-multiplication** by the rightmost (innermost) frame's rotation.
- ☐ **Order matters**: $R_1 R_2 \neq R_2 R_1$ for rotations about *different* axes.
- ☐ Same-axis rotations **commute** and **add angles**: $R_Z(\alpha) R_Z(\beta) = R_Z(\alpha + \beta)$.
- ☐ Inverse of a fixed-angle rotation: reverse the order and negate all angles.
- ☐ **Always verify** orthogonality and determinant at the end of any 14-mark KTU problem.
- ☐ Columns of $^A_BR$ are the unit vectors of frame $\{B\}$ expressed in frame $\{A\}$.
- ☐ Free vectors are used for **forces, moments, angular velocities** — quantities that don't depend on application point.
- ☐ Position vectors are used for **end-effector locations, joint offsets** — quantities tied to specific reference frames.
- ☐ The **homogeneous transform** combines rotation and translation in a single $4 \times 4$ matrix for compact manipulation.
- ☐ In KTU problems, **always show your work step-by-step** — partial credit depends on clearly written intermediate steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition of Rotation Matrices - Euler Angles - YZY Angles - Roll-Pitch-Yaw Angles - Angle and Axis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT631 - INDUSTRIAL MANIPULATORS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composition of Rotation Matrices - Euler Angles - YZY Angles - Roll-Pitch-Yaw Angles - Angle and Axis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Composition of Rotation Matrices

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
The composition of rotation matrices refers to the algebraic product of two or more basic rotation matrices (each representing a rotation about a principal axis) to obtain a resultant homogeneous transformation matrix that describes the net orientation of a rigid body in 3D space. If $R_1$ describes a rotation from frame $\{1\}$ to $\{2\}$, and $R_2$ describes a rotation from $\{2\}$ to $\{3\}$, the composed rotation is $R_3 = R_2 R_1$ (note the **pre-multiplication order**, which is non-commutative).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** Rotation matrix composition is **non-commutative** ($R_1 R_2 \neq R_2 R_1$). The order of multiplication directly encodes the sequence of physical rotations, making it the cornerstone of manipulator kinematic modelling.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Book on a Table:**
Imagine placing a closed book flat on a table, then rotating it 90° around the **vertical (Z) axis**, followed by 90° around the **horizontal (X) axis**. The final orientation of the book depends critically on the order. If you reverse the sequence, the book's spine will not end up pointing in the same direction. This is precisely the non-commutativity that governs every joint motion of an industrial robot arm — the path matters, not just the endpoints.

---

### 1.2 Euler Angles (General Definition)

**Formal Definition:**
Euler Angles are a set of **three angular parameters** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ used to uniquely describe any arbitrary orientation of a rigid body (or coordinate frame) with respect to a reference frame, by specifying a sequence of three successive rotations about **principal axes**. There are exactly **12 unique Euler angle conventions**, classified into:

- **Proper Euler Angles** — first and third rotations share the same axis (e.g., **YZY**, ZYZ, XYX).
- **Tait–Bryan Angles** — all three rotations occur about **different** principal axes (e.g., **Roll–Pitch–Yaw / ZYX**, XYZ, ZXY).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This module explicitly targets the **YZY** convention (Proper Euler) and the **Roll–Pitch–Yaw (ZYX)** convention (Tait–Bryan).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Gyroscope:**
Imagine a spinning gyroscope mounted on a gimbal. You can tilt it around the inner gimbal axis ($\phi$), then swing it around the middle gimbal axis ($\theta$), and finally twist it around the outer gimbal axis ($\psi$). The final orientation of the gyroscope's spin axis is fully described by these three successive rotations — this is the Euler angle representation.

---

### 1.3 YZY Euler Angles

**Formal Definition:**
The YZY convention represents an orientation as **three successive rotations**: first by angle $\phi$ about the **Y-axis**, then by angle $\theta$ about the (rotated) **Z'-axis**, and finally by angle $\psi$ about the (twice-rotated) **Y''-axis**. The composed rotation matrix is:

$$R_{YZY}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_Y(\phi) \, R_Z(\theta) \, R_Y(\psi)$$

**Conceptual Analogy — The Wrist of a Robot Arm:**
Consider the wrist of a 6-DOF industrial manipulator. The first rotation $\phi$ (about Y) orients the wrist plane, the second rotation $\theta$ (about Z) twists the wrist, and the third rotation $\psi$ (about Y) re-orients the gripper's final roll. This "yaw–twist–roll" pattern is exactly the YZY sequence.

---

### 1.4 Roll–Pitch–Yaw (RPY / ZYX) Angles

**Formal Definition:**
The Roll–Pitch–Yaw convention uses the **ZYX Tait–Bryan sequence**: first a **roll** $\phi$ about the X-axis, then a **pitch** $\theta$ about the Y'-axis, and finally a **yaw** $\psi$ about the Z''-axis. The composed rotation matrix is:

$$R_{RPY}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_Z(\psi) \, R_Y(\theta) \, R_X(\phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RPY Memory Trick:** Reading from **right to left** in the matrix product, the order is **X → Y → Z**, matching the aerospace convention "roll about nose-to-tail axis, pitch about wing axis, yaw about vertical axis."

**Conceptual Analogy — Airplane Attitude:**
The orientation of an aircraft relative to Earth's ground frame is described by:
- **Roll** ($\phi$) — banking the wings (rotation about the fuselage X-axis).
- **Pitch** ($\theta$) — nose up/down (rotation about the wing Y-axis).
- **Yaw** ($\psi$) — turning left/right (rotation about the vertical Z-axis).

---

### 1.5 Angle and Axis Representation (Rodrigues' Rotation Theorem)

**Formal Definition:**
Any rotation in 3D space can be expressed as a **single rotation by an angle $\theta$ about an arbitrary unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (k_x, k_y, k_z)$** through the origin (Rodrigues' Theorem, 1840). The rotation matrix is:

$$R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = I + \sin\theta \, K + (1-\cos\theta) \, K^2$$

where $K$ is the **skew-symmetric matrix** (cross-product operator) of $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -k_z & k_y \\ k_z & 0 & -k_x \\ -k_y & k_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conceptual Analogy — The Door Hinge:**
The orientation of any door, no matter how it has been rotated in space, can always be described by a single "swing" — the angle the door has opened, and the axis (a line in space) about which the swing occurred. The hinge line is the axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$; the opening angle is $\theta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universality:** Rodrigues' theorem guarantees that **any orientation** in SO(3) can be reached by a single rotation about some axis — making this the most compact orientation representation (4 parameters: 1 axis-angle pair, but constrained).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Framework

#### (a) Special Orthogonal Group SO(3)
- All valid rotation matrices belong to the **Lie group SO(3)**, defined by:
  $R \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ such that $R^T R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$.
- This imposes **6 orthogonality constraints** on 9 elements, leaving **3 degrees of freedom** — exactly the number of independent rotations in space.

#### (b) Elementary Rotation Matrices (Building Blocks)

Each principal-axis rotation uses a **3×3 matrix** derived from projecting the rotated basis vectors onto the original basis:

$$R_X(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_Y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_Z(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### (c) Composition Algebra
- A sequence of $n$ rotations about different axes is represented by ordered matrix multiplication.
- **Pre-multiplication** $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{intermediate}} \, R_{\text{current}}$ represents a new rotation described in the *current* frame being expressed in the *intermediate* frame.
- **Post-multiplication** $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{current}} \, R_{\text{intermediate}}$ represents a new rotation in the same fixed reference frame.

#### (d) Why Three Angles Are Necessary
- A point in 3D space requires 3 coordinates (x, y, z) for **position**.
- An orientation requires **3 parameters** for the orientation part of the **pose** — these are the three Euler/Tait–Bryan angles or, equivalently, the angle-axis pair (with a unit-vector constraint).

#### (e) Engineering Utility
- **Robot forward kinematics** — DH-parameter-based composition of joint rotations.
- **CNC machine toolpath planning** — orientation of cutting tool at each waypoint.
- **Aerospace navigation** — aircraft and satellite attitude control.
- **Computer graphics** — camera and object orientation rendering.
- **IMU sensor fusion** — converting gyroscope readings to orientation updates.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **all exam-critical formulas** for this topic. Memorize the matrix elements row-by-row; KTU board examiners frequently ask for explicit term-by-term multiplication.

**Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference Sheet**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Parameters | Valid Range |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Rotation about X-axis | $R_X(\alpha)$ as above | $\alpha \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | All real $\alpha$ |
| 2 | Rotation about Y-axis | $R_Y(\beta)$ as above | $\beta \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | All real $\beta$ |
| 3 | Rotation about Z-axis | $R_Z(\gamma)$ as above | $\gamma \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | All real $\gamma$ |
| 4 | Orthogonality test | $R^T R = I$ | $\det R = +1$ | Identity check |
| 5 | YZY Euler composition | $R_{YZY} = R_Y(\phi) R_Z(\theta) R_Y(\psi)$ | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ | $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ (Proper Euler) |
| 6 | YZY $r_{11}$ element | $c\phi c\theta c\psi - s\phi s\psi$ | Uses shorthand $c=\cos, s=\sin$ | — |
| 7 | YZY $r_{22}$ element | $\cos\theta$ | Direct extraction | — |
| 8 | YZY $r_{12}$ element | $-c\phi s\theta$ | — | — |
| 9 | RPY composition | $R_{RPY} = R_Z(\psi) R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi)$ | $\phi$ = roll, $\theta$ = pitch, $\psi$ = yaw | $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ |
| 10 | RPY $r_{31}$ element | $-\sin\theta$ | Direct extraction | — |
| 11 | RPY $r_{33}$ element | $\cos\theta \cos\phi$ | — | — |
| 12 | RPY $r_{11}$ element | $\cos\psi \cos\theta$ | — | — |
| 13 | Rodrigues' formula | $R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = I + s\theta K + (1-c\theta) K^2$ | $s\theta = \sin\theta$, $c\theta = \cos\theta$ | $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ |
| 14 | Skew-symmetric operator | $K$ matrix of $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (k_x, k_y, k_z)$ | $\vert\vert \hat{\mathbf{k}} \vert\vert_2 = 1$ | Unit axis |
| 15 | Inverse of $R$ | $R^{-1} = R^T$ | $\det R = +1$ | Always holds |
| 16 | Rotation composition rule | $R_{total} = R_n R_{n-1} \cdots R_2 R_1$ | Right-to-left = chronological | — |
| 17 | Trace relation to angle | $\mathrm{tr}(R) = 1 + 2\cos\theta$ | Extracts rotation angle | — |
| 18 | Identity matrix | $I = \mathrm{diag}(1,1,1)$ | No rotation | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Shorthand Convention Used Throughout:** $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$, $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$. This saves substantial writing time in KTU board examinations.

---

### 2.3 Comparison: Proper Euler vs. Tait–Bryan

| Feature | Proper Euler (YZY, ZYZ, …) | Tait–Bryan (RPY/ZYX, XYZ, …) |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------------------|
| First and third axes | **Same** axis | All **different** axes |
| Middle angle range | $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ | $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ |
| Singularity condition | $\sin\theta = 0$ | $\cos\theta = 0$ |
| Common applications | Spinning tops, wrist joints | Aircraft, mobile robots, cameras |
| Matrix symmetry | Special structure | Generally no symmetry |

---

### 2.4 Engineering Application Scenarios

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Implementation:** In the **KUKA KR 16 R2010** industrial manipulator used in automotive welding cells, the wrist joint sequence typically follows the **ZYZ** or **YZY** Euler convention, while AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) localization uses **RPY** because of its direct correspondence with inertial sensor (IMU) outputs.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the YZY Rotation Matrix

**Problem Setup:** Compute the explicit 3×3 rotation matrix $R_{YZY} = R_Y(\phi) \, R_Z(\theta) \, R_Y(\psi)$.

**Step 1 — Write down the three elementary rotation matrices.**

$$R_Y(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi & 0 & s\phi \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\phi & 0 & c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_Z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & -s\theta & 0 \\ s\theta & c\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_Y(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi & 0 & s\psi \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\psi & 0 & c\psi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intermediate product $A = R_Y(\phi) \, R_Z(\theta)$.**

Perform element-wise multiplication. For each row $i$ and column $j$, compute the dot product of row $i$ of $R_Y(\phi)$ with column $j$ of $R_Z(\theta)$.

**Row 1 of A** (using row $[c\phi, 0, s\phi]$):

$$a_{11} = c\phi \cdot c\theta + 0 \cdot s\theta + s\phi \cdot 0 = c\phi c\theta$$

$$a_{12} = c\phi \cdot (-s\theta) + 0 \cdot c\theta + s\phi \cdot 0 = -c\phi s\theta$$

$$a_{13} = c\phi \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 + s\phi \cdot 1 = s\phi$$

**Row 2 of A** (using row $[0, 1, 0]$):

$$a_{21} = 0 \cdot c\theta + 1 \cdot s\theta + 0 \cdot 0 = s\theta$$

$$a_{22} = 0 \cdot (-s\theta) + 1 \cdot c\theta + 0 \cdot 0 = c\theta$$

$$a_{23} = 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 1 = 0$$

**Row 3 of A** (using row $[-s\phi, 0, c\phi]$):

$$a_{31} = -s\phi \cdot c\theta + 0 \cdot s\theta + c\phi \cdot 0 = -s\phi c\theta$$

$$a_{32} = -s\phi \cdot (-s\theta) + 0 \cdot c\theta + c\phi \cdot 0 = s\phi s\theta$$

$$a_{33} = -s\phi \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 + c\phi \cdot 1 = c\phi$$

So:

$$A = R_Y(\phi) R_Z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi c\theta & -c\phi s\theta & s\phi \\ s\theta & c\theta & 0 \\ -s\phi c\theta & s\phi s\theta & c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_{YZY} = A \, R_Y(\psi)$ where $R_Y(\psi)$ has columns as above.**

**Row 1 of $R_{YZY}$** (using row $[c\phi c\theta, -c\phi s\theta, s\phi]$):

$$r_{11} = c\phi c\theta \cdot c\psi + (-c\phi s\theta) \cdot 0 + s\phi \cdot (-s\psi)$$

$$r_{11} = c\phi c\theta c\psi - s\phi s\psi$$

$$r_{12} = c\phi c\theta \cdot 0 + (-c\phi s\theta) \cdot 1 + s\phi \cdot 0 = -c\phi s\theta$$

$$r_{13} = c\phi c\theta \cdot s\psi + (-c\phi s\theta) \cdot 0 + s\phi \cdot c\psi$$

$$r_{13} = c\phi c\theta s\psi + s\phi c\psi$$

**Row 2 of $R_{YZY}$** (using row $[s\theta, c\theta, 0]$):

$$r_{21} = s\theta \cdot c\psi + c\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-s\psi) = s\theta c\psi$$

$$r_{22} = s\theta \cdot 0 + c\theta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 = c\theta$$

$$r_{23} = s\theta \cdot s\psi + c\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot c\psi = s\theta s\psi$$

**Row 3 of $R_{YZY}$** (using row $[-s\phi c\theta, s\phi s\theta, c\phi]$):

$$r_{31} = -s\phi c\theta \cdot c\psi + s\phi s\theta \cdot 0 + c\phi \cdot (-s\psi)$$

$$r_{31} = -s\phi c\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi$$

$$r_{32} = -s\phi c\theta \cdot 0 + s\phi s\theta \cdot 1 + c\phi \cdot 0 = s\phi s\theta$$

$$r_{33} = -s\phi c\theta \cdot s\psi + s\phi s\theta \cdot 0 + c\phi \cdot c\psi$$

$$r_{33} = -s\phi c\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi$$

**Final YZY Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{YZY}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi c\theta c\psi - s\phi s\psi & -c\phi s\theta & c\phi c\theta s\psi + s\phi c\psi \\ s\theta c\psi & c\theta & s\theta s\psi \\ -s\phi c\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & s\phi s\theta & -s\phi c\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:** Check orthogonality $R^T R = I$ (computationally heavy but a key KTU board verification step).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Roll–Pitch–Yaw (RPY) Matrix

**Problem Setup:** Compute $R_{RPY} = R_Z(\psi) \, R_Y(\theta) \, R_X(\phi)$.

**Step 1 — Write down the three matrices.**

$$R_X(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ 0 & s\phi & c\phi \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_Y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & 0 & s\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -s\theta & 0 & c\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$R_Z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi & -s\psi & 0 \\ s\psi & c\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intermediate product $B = R_Y(\theta) \, R_X(\phi)$.**

**Row 1 of B** (using row $[c\theta, 0, s\theta]$):

$$b_{11} = c\theta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + s\theta \cdot 0 = c\theta$$

$$b_{12} = c\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot c\phi + s\theta \cdot s\phi = s\theta s\phi$$

$$b_{13} = c\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-s\phi) + s\theta \cdot c\phi = s\theta c\phi$$

**Row 2 of B** (using row $[0, 1, 0]$):

$$b_{21} = 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 = 0$$

$$b_{22} = 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot c\phi + 0 \cdot s\phi = c\phi$$

$$b_{23} = 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-s\phi) + 0 \cdot c\phi = -s\phi$$

**Row 3 of B** (using row $[-s\theta, 0, c\theta]$):

$$b_{31} = -s\theta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + c\theta \cdot 0 = -s\theta$$

$$b_{32} = -s\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot c\phi + c\theta \cdot s\phi = c\theta s\phi$$

$$b_{33} = -s\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-s\phi) + c\theta \cdot c\phi = c\theta c\phi$$

So:

$$B = R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & s\theta s\phi & s\theta c\phi \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_{RPY} = R_Z(\psi) \, B$.**

**Row 1 of $R_{RPY}$** (using row $[c\psi, -s\psi, 0]$):

$$r_{11} = c\psi \cdot c\theta + (-s\psi) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-s\theta) = c\psi c\theta$$

$$r_{12} = c\psi \cdot s\theta s\phi + (-s\psi) \cdot c\phi + 0 \cdot c\theta s\phi$$

$$r_{12} = c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi$$

$$r_{13} = c\psi \cdot s\theta c\phi + (-s\psi) \cdot (-s\phi) + 0 \cdot c\theta c\phi$$

$$r_{13} = c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi$$

**Row 2 of $R_{RPY}$** (using row $[s\psi, c\psi, 0]$):

$$r_{21} = s\psi \cdot c\theta + c\psi \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-s\theta) = s\psi c\theta$$

$$r_{22} = s\psi \cdot s\theta s\phi + c\psi \cdot c\phi + 0 \cdot c\theta s\phi$$

$$r_{22} = s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi$$

$$r_{23} = s\psi \cdot s\theta c\phi + c\psi \cdot (-s\phi) + 0 \cdot c\theta c\phi$$

$$r_{23} = s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi$$

**Row 3 of $R_{RPY}$** (using row $[0, 0, 1]$):

$$r_{31} = 0 \cdot c\theta + 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-s\theta) = -s\theta$$

$$r_{32} = 0 \cdot s\theta s\phi + 0 \cdot c\phi + 1 \cdot c\theta s\phi = c\theta s\phi$$

$$r_{33} = 0 \cdot s\theta c\phi + 0 \cdot (-s\phi) + 1 \cdot c\theta c\phi = c\theta c\phi$$

**Final RPY Result:**

$$\boxed{R_{RPY}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:** When $\phi = \theta = \psi = 0$, all matrix elements reduce to the identity matrix $I$. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Rodrigues' Rotation Formula (Angle–Axis)

**Goal:** Derive $R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = I + \sin\theta \, K + (1 - \cos\theta) \, K^2$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Decompose a vector $\mathbf{v}$ into components parallel and perpendicular to the axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$.**

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_{\parallel} + \mathbf{v}_{\perp}$$

where:
$$\mathbf{v}_{\parallel} = (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \, \hat{\mathbf{k}}, \quad \mathbf{v}_{\perp} = \mathbf{v} - (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \, \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

**Step 2 — Rotation leaves $\mathbf{v}_{\parallel}$ unchanged but rotates $\mathbf{v}_{\perp}$ in the plane perpendicular to $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ by angle $\theta$.**

Let $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = \mathbf{v}_{\perp} / \vert\vert \mathbf{v}_{\perp} \vert\vert$ and $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{u}}$. Then:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\perp, \text{rotated}} = \mathbf{v}_{\perp} \cos\theta + (\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}_{\perp}) \sin\theta$$

So:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{rotated}} = \mathbf{v}_{\parallel} + \mathbf{v}_{\perp} \cos\theta + (\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}_{\perp}) \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Express everything in matrix form.**

Since $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v} = K \mathbf{v}$ (definition of the skew-symmetric matrix), we have:

$$\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}_{\perp} = K \mathbf{v}_{\perp} = K(\mathbf{v} - (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \hat{\mathbf{k}}) = K\mathbf{v} - (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) K\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

Since $K\hat{\mathbf{k}} = \hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{k}} = \mathbf{0}$, this simplifies to $K \mathbf{v}$.

Thus:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{rotated}} = (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \hat{\mathbf{k}} + (\mathbf{v} - (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cos\theta + (K\mathbf{v}) \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Group terms.**

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{rotated}} = \mathbf{v} \cos\theta + (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \hat{\mathbf{k}} (1 - \cos\theta) + K\mathbf{v} \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Convert the projection $(\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ to matrix form.**

Note that $(\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \hat{\mathbf{k}} = (\hat{\mathbf{k}} \hat{\mathbf{k}}^T) \mathbf{v}$, where $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \hat{\mathbf{k}}^T$ is an outer product.

Also, observe the identity: $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \hat{\mathbf{k}}^T = I + K^2$, because:

$$K^2 = \hat{\mathbf{k}} \hat{\mathbf{k}}^T - I$$

This is verifiable by direct matrix multiplication of $K$ with itself.

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{rotated}} = \mathbf{v} \cos\theta + (I + K^2)\mathbf{v}(1 - \cos\theta) + K\mathbf{v}\sin\theta$$

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{rotated}} = \left[ \cos\theta \, I + (1-\cos\theta)(I + K^2) + \sin\theta \, K \right] \mathbf{v}$$

$$\mathbf{v}_{\text{rotated}} = \left[ I + \sin\theta \, K + (1 - \cos\theta) K^2 \right] \mathbf{v}$$

**Final Rodrigues' Result:**

$$\boxed{R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = I + \sin\theta \, K + (1-\cos\theta) \, K^2}$$

**Explicit Matrix Form:**

$$R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = \begin{bmatrix} k_x^2(1-c\theta)+c\theta & k_x k_y(1-c\theta) - k_z s\theta & k_x k_z(1-c\theta) + k_y s\theta \\ k_x k_y(1-c\theta) + k_z s\theta & k_y^2(1-c\theta)+c\theta & k_y k_z(1-c\theta) - k_x s\theta \\ k_x k_z(1-c\theta) - k_y s\theta & k_y k_z(1-c\theta) + k_x s\theta & k_z^2(1-c\theta)+c\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification for Special Case:** If $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (0, 0, 1)$ and $\theta = \alpha$, then $K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $K^2 = \mathrm{diag}(-1, -1, 0)$.

Plugging in:
$$R = I + s\alpha K + (1-c\alpha) K^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - s\alpha \cdot 0 + (1-c\alpha)(-1) & 0 - s\alpha & 0 \\ s\alpha & 1-c\alpha)(-1) + c\alpha \end{bmatrix} \ldots$$

Simplifying recovers $R_Z(\alpha)$. ✓

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style Problem)

**Problem:** Given $\phi = 30°, \theta = 45°, \psi = 60°$, compute $R_{YZY}$.

**Solution Setup:** Use the derived formula. Numerical values:
- $c\phi = \cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$, $s\phi = 0.5$
- $c\theta = \cos 45° = \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.707$, $s\theta \approx 0.707$
- $c\psi = \cos 60° = 0.5$, $s\psi = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$

**Computing $r_{11}$:**
$$r_{11} = c\phi c\theta c\psi - s\phi s\psi = (0.866)(0.707)(0.5) - (0.5)(0.866)$$
$$r_{11} = 0.3062 - 0.4330 = -0.1268$$

**Computing $r_{22}$:**
$$r_{22} = c\theta = 0.707$$

**Computing $r_{33}$:**
$$r_{33} = -s\phi c\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi = -(0.5)(0.707)(0.866) + (0.866)(0.5)$$
$$r_{33} = -0.3062 + 0.4330 = 0.1268$$

The complete numerical matrix is:

$$R_{YZY} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.1268 & -0.6124 & 0.7803 \\ 0.3536 & 0.7071 & 0.6124 \\ -0.9268 & 0.3536 & -0.1268 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Sanity Check:** $\det(R) \approx 1$ ✓ (compute if needed in exam).

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 YZY Rotation Sequence — Functional Flow Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the **frame transformation sequence** for the YZY Euler angle convention. Each block represents a coordinate frame transition, with the elementary rotation axis labelled.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Reference Frame 0] -->|Ry phi rotation 1| N1[Frame 1 after Y rotation]
    N1 -->|Rz theta rotation 2| N2[Frame 2 after Z rotation]
    N2 -->|Ry psi rotation 3| N3[Frame 3 Final YZY orientation]

    N1 -. axis .-> AX1[Y prime axis]
    N2 -. axis .-> AX2[Z double prime axis]
    N3 -. axis .-> AX3[Y double prime axis]

    style N0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style N1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style N2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style N3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style AX1 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style AX2 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style AX3 fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
```

**Interpretation:** Frame 0 → Frame 1: rotate by $\phi$ around the fixed Y-axis. Frame 1 → Frame 2: rotate by $\theta$ around the **current Z'-axis** of Frame 1. Frame 2 → Frame 3: rotate by $\psi$ around the **current Y''-axis** of Frame 2.

```mermaid

    N1A -->|Ry pitch theta| N2A[Frame 2 Y rotation]
    N2A -->|Rz yaw psi| N3A[Frame 3 Final RPY orientation]

    N0A -. aircraft .-> AV1[Roll about fuselage]
    N1A -. aircraft .-> AV2[Pitch about wing]

    style N0A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1

    style N3A fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style AV1 fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242
    style AV2 fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242
    style AV3 fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242
```

**Interpretation:** In aerospace applications, the **first** rotation is around the **aircraft's longitudinal (X) axis** (roll), the **second** around the **lateral (Y) axis** (pitch), and the **third** around the **vertical (Z) axis** (yaw). The matrix product $R_Z R_Y R_X$ reads right-to-left as the physical rotation sequence.

---

### 4.3 Angle–Axis Rotation Concept Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Unit Axis vector kx ky kz] --> NORM[Normalization step k squared equals 1]
    INP --> SKEW[Construct Skew symmetric matrix K]
    ANG[Rotation angle theta in radians] --> SIN[Compute sin theta]
    ANG --> COS[Compute 1 minus cos theta]

    SKEW --> FORM[Apply Rodrigues formula]
    SIN --> FORM
    COS --> FORM
    NORM --> FORM

    FORM --> RESULT[3x3 Rotation Matrix R]

    FORM -. verification .-> VER[Check trace R equals 1 plus 2 cos theta]

    style INP fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style ANG fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style NORM fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style SKEW fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style SIN fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style COS fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style FORM fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style RESULT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style VER fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

**Interpretation:** The flow shows the input requirements (axis + angle) and the computational pipeline producing the rotation matrix. The verification node confirms the trace identity.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Comparison: Proper Euler vs. Tait–Bryan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[3D Orientation Representation Methods]

    TOP --> PROPER[Proper Euler Angles]
    TOP --> TB[Tait Bryan Angles]
    TOP --> AXIS[Angle Axis Rodrigues]

    PROPER --> YZY[YZY first equals last axis Y]
    PROPER --> ZYZ[ZYZ first equals last axis Z]
    PROPER --> XYX[XYX first equals last axis X]

    TB --> RPY[RPY ZYX Aerospace convention]
    TB --> XYZ[XYZ Convention]
    TB --> ZXY[ZXY Convention]

    AXIS --> ROD[Rodrigues 4 parameters 1 axis plus 1 angle]
    AXIS --> QUAT[Quaternions 4 parameters unit norm]

    style TOP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style PROPER fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style TB fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style AXIS fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

---

### 4.5 Functional Architecture: Industrial Manipulator Orientation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    JNT[Joint Angles q1 q2 q3 q4 q5 q6] --> DH[DH Parameter Table]
    DH --> TRANS[Homogeneous Transformations A1 to A6]
    TRANS --> COMP[Sequential Matrix Composition]
    COMP --> ORIENT[End Effector Orientation R 3x3]
    COMP --> POS[End Effector Position p 3x1]

    ORIENT --> EULER[Extract Euler Angles for visualization]
    ORIENT --> AXISANG[Convert to Angle Axis if needed]
    ORIENT --> CTRL[Motion Controller Trajectory]

    style JNT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style DH fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style TRANS fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style COMP fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style ORIENT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style POS fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style EULER fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style AXISANG fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style CTRL fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows how joint angles flow through the DH-parameter-based forward kinematics pipeline to produce the end-effector orientation matrix $R$. The orientation can then be re-expressed in any of the representations discussed in this module.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** State Euler's Rotation Theorem. What is the minimum number of parameters required to describe the orientation of a rigid body in 3D space?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Euler's Rotation Theorem states that any displacement of a rigid body in 3D space, with one point fixed, is equivalent to a **single rotation about some axis passing through that fixed point**. The minimum number of independent parameters required to describe orientation is **three (3)** — for example, the three Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$, or the angle-axis pair $(\theta, k_x, k_y)$ with the constraint $k_x^2 + k_y^2 + k_z^2 = 1$. The 3×3 rotation matrix itself contains 9 elements but only 3 are independent due to the 6 orthogonality constraints of SO(3). **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 Model]** Differentiate between Proper Euler Angles and Tait–Bryan Angles. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The differentiation is based on the axis sequence of the three successive rotations:

| Aspect | Proper Euler Angles | Tait–Bryan Angles |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------|
| First and third axes | **Same** axis (e.g., Y-Y, Z-Z) | **Different** axes (e.g., X-Y-Z) |
| Middle angle range | $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ | $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ |
| Example | **YZY**, ZYZ, XYX | **Roll-Pitch-Yaw (ZYX)**, XYZ |

**Example of Proper Euler:** YZY — used in spinning top analysis and some robot wrist designs. **Example of Tait–Bryan:** Roll-Pitch-Yaw — used in aerospace navigation and IMU sensor fusion. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When composing rotation matrices, students commonly **lose 2-3 marks** by:
> 1. Forgetting the **non-commutative order** and writing $R_Y R_Z R_Y$ instead of using the correct frame-convention sequence.
> 2. Skipping intermediate steps and jumping directly to the final matrix, missing the structured derivation marks.
> 3. Confusing the shorthand $c\alpha$ with the full $\cos\alpha$ form when the exam explicitly asks for derivation.
> 4. Failing to verify orthogonality or compute the determinant as a sanity check.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the complete YZY rotation matrix $R_{YZY}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_Y(\phi) R_Z(\theta) R_Y(\psi)$. Show all intermediate matrix multiplications explicitly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For $\phi = 30°, \theta = 45°, \psi = 60°$, compute the numerical value of each element of $R_{YZY}$. Verify that the determinant equals +1. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write down the three elementary rotation matrices $R_Y(\phi), R_Z(\theta), R_Y(\psi)$ in expanded form.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the intermediate product $A = R_Y(\phi) R_Z(\theta)$ by performing all nine scalar multiplications. The resulting intermediate matrix is:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi c\theta & -c\phi s\theta & s\phi \\ s\theta & c\theta & 0 \\ -s\phi c\theta & s\phi s\theta & c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Multiply $A$ by $R_Y(\psi)$ on the right. Compute each of the nine elements:

- Row 1: $r_{11} = c\phi c\theta c\psi - s\phi s\psi$, $r_{12} = -c\phi s\theta$, $r_{13} = c\phi c\theta s\psi + s\phi c\psi$
- Row 2: $r_{21} = s\theta c\psi$, $r_{22} = c\theta$, $r_{23} = s\theta s\psi$
- Row 3: $r_{31} = -s\phi c\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi$, $r_{32} = s\phi s\theta$, $r_{33} = -s\phi c\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Present the final composed matrix:

$$R_{YZY} = \begin{bmatrix} c\phi c\theta c\psi - s\phi s\psi & -c\phi s\theta & c\phi c\theta s\psi + s\phi c\psi \\ s\theta c\psi & c\theta & s\theta s\psi \\ -s\phi c\theta c\psi - c\phi s\psi & s\phi s\theta & -s\phi c\theta s\psi + c\phi c\psi \end{bmatrix}$$

**[7 Marks Total]**

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** List the trigonometric values:
- $c\phi = \cos 30° = 0.8660$, $s\phi = 0.5000$
- $c\theta = \cos 45° = 0.7071$, $s\theta = 0.7071$
- $c\psi = \cos 60° = 0.5000$, $s\psi = 0.8660$

**Step 2 (4 marks):** Compute each element with two-decimal precision:

| Element | Calculation | Result |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| $r_{11}$ | $(0.866)(0.707)(0.5) - (0.5)(0.866)$ | $-0.127$ |
| $r_{12}$ | $-(0.866)(0.707)$ | $-0.612$ |
| $r_{13}$ | $(0.866)(0.707)(0.866) + (0.5)(0.5)$ | $0.780$ |
| $r_{21}$ | $(0.707)(0.5)$ | $0.354$ |
| $r_{22}$ | $0.707$ | $0.707$ |
| $r_{23}$ | $(0.707)(0.866)$ | $0.612$ |
| $r_{31}$ | $-(0.5)(0.707)(0.5) - (0.866)(0.866)$ | $-0.927$ |
| $r_{32}$ | $(0.5)(0.707)$ | $0.354$ |
| $r_{33}$ | $-(0.5)(0.707)(0.866) + (0.866)(0.5)$ | $0.127$ |

$$R_{YZY} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.127 & -0.612 & 0.780 \\ 0.354 & 0.707 & 0.612 \\ -0.927 & 0.354 & 0.127 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the determinant using cofactor expansion along Row 1:

$$\det(R) = (-0.127)[(0.707)(0.127) - (0.612)(0.354)] - (-0.612)[(0.354)(0.127) - (0.612)(-0.927)] + (0.780)[(0.354)(0.354) - (0.707)(-0.927)]$$

$$\det(R) = (-0.127)[-0.127] - (-0.612)[0.045 + 0.567] + (0.780)[0.125 + 0.655]$$

$$\det(R) = 0.0161 + 0.3747 + 0.6084 \approx 0.999 \approx 1$$

✓ **Determinant equals +1**, confirming the matrix is a valid rotation in SO(3). **[7 Marks Total]**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Roll-Pitch-Yaw rotation matrix $R_{RPY}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_Z(\psi) R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi)$. Show all intermediate matrix multiplications explicitly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive Rodrigues' rotation formula $R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = I + \sin\theta K + (1-\cos\theta)K^2$ from the vector decomposition $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_\parallel + \mathbf{v}_\perp$, where $K$ is the skew-symmetric matrix of the unit axis $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — RPY Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write down $R_X(\phi), R_Y(\theta), R_Z(\psi)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $B = R_Y(\theta) R_X(\phi)$:

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta & s\theta s\phi & s\theta c\phi \\ 0 & c\phi & -s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Compute $R_{RPY} = R_Z(\psi) B$ element by element:
- $r_{11} = c\psi c\theta$
- $r_{12} = c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi$
- $r_{13} = c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi$
- $r_{21} = s\psi c\theta$
- $r_{22} = s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi$
- $r_{23} = s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi$
- $r_{31} = -s\theta$
- $r_{32} = c\theta s\phi$
- $r_{33} = c\theta c\phi$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final composed matrix:

$$R_{RPY} = \begin{bmatrix} c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\ s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\ -s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**[7 Marks Total]**

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — Rodrigues' Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Decompose vector $\mathbf{v}$ as $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}_\parallel + \mathbf{v}_\perp$ where $\mathbf{v}_\parallel = (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}})\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\mathbf{v}_\perp = \mathbf{v} - (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}})\hat{\mathbf{k}}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** After rotation by $\theta$ about $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$, the parallel component is unchanged and the perpendicular component rotates in the plane perpendicular to $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$:

$$\mathbf{v}_\text{rot} = \mathbf{v}_\parallel + \mathbf{v}_\perp \cos\theta + (\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}_\perp)\sin\theta$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute and simplify. Using $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{v}_\perp = K\mathbf{v}$ (since $K\hat{\mathbf{k}} = 0$):

$$\mathbf{v}_\text{rot} = \mathbf{v} \cos\theta + (\mathbf{v} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}})\hat{\mathbf{k}}(1-\cos\theta) + K\mathbf{v}\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the identity $\hat{\mathbf{k}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}^T = I + K^2$ and factor out $\mathbf{v}$:

$$\mathbf{v}_\text{rot} = \left[ \cos\theta \cdot I + (1-\cos\theta)(I + K^2) + \sin\theta \cdot K \right] \mathbf{v}$$

$$\boxed{R(\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \theta) = I + \sin\theta \cdot K + (1-\cos\theta) K^2}$$

**[7 Marks Total]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Topic: Composition of Rotation Matrices, Euler Angles, YZY, RPY, Angle–Axis**

**Core Conceptual Points:**
- [ ] Rotation matrices belong to **SO(3)** — orthogonal with determinant +1.
- [ ] Rotation composition is **non-commutative** ($R_1 R_2 \neq R_2 R_1$ in general).
- [ ] Matrix multiplication order **right-to-left = chronological rotation order**.
- [ ] Exactly **12 Euler angle conventions** exist: 6 Proper Euler + 6 Tait–Bryan.
- [ ] **Proper Euler** = first and last axes identical (e.g., YZY).
- [ ] **Tait–Bryan** = all three axes different (e.g., ZYX for RPY).

**Key Matrices to Memorize:**
- [ ] $R_X(\alpha), R_Y(\beta), R_Z(\gamma)$ — elementary rotations.
- [ ] **YZY result** — middle row has the form $[s\theta c\psi, \; c\theta, \; s\theta s\psi]$.
- [ ] **RPY result** — middle-left element $r_{31} = -s\theta$ is a clean identifier.
- [ ] **Rodrigues' matrix** — diagonal terms are $k_i^2(1-c\theta) + c\theta$.
- [ ] **Skew-symmetric matrix** $K$ for $\hat{\mathbf{k}} = (k_x, k_y, k_z)$.

**Numerical Shortcuts:**
- [ ] $c\alpha = \cos\alpha$, $s\alpha = \sin\alpha$ — always use shorthand in derivations.
- [ ] Standard angles: $30°, 45°, 60°, 90°$ — memorize both sin and cos values.
- [ ] $\cos\theta = r_{22}$ in YZY (direct extraction).
- [ ] $\cos\theta = -r_{31}$ in RPY (direct extraction of pitch).

**Verification Procedures:**
- [ ] $\det(R) = +1$ for every valid rotation matrix.
- [ ] $R^T R = I$ (orthogonality check).
- [ ] $\mathrm{tr}(R) = 1 + 2\cos\theta$ for angle extraction.
- [ ] Each row and each column of $R$ is a unit vector.

**Engineering Applications:**
- [ ] **YZY** — spinning tops, gimbal-mounted sensors, robot wrist joints.
- [ ] **RPY** — aircraft attitude, IMU/INS, mobile robots, camera gimbals.
- [ ] **Rodrigues' formula** — minimal representation, quaternion foundation, single-axis rotations.

**Exam Strategy:**
- [ ] Always state **frame convention** before composing matrices.
- [ ] Show **all intermediate products** to earn full marks (don't skip to the answer).
- [ ] Verify $\det = +1$ in numerical problems for partial credit.
- [ ] Sketch the **axis rotation sequence** in a small diagram to clarify the physical interpretation.
- [ ] For angle–axis problems, **normalise the axis vector** first if not already unit length.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT631 - INDUSTRIAL MANIPULATORS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Kinematic Chain** is defined as an assembly of rigid bodies (links) connected together by means of kinematic pairs (joints) such that each link moves relative to the others in a constrained, predictable manner. Mathematically, it is a system where relative motion between consecutive links is governed by the type of joint (pair) connecting them, transferring motion and force from one link to the next.

In the context of **Industrial Manipulators**, a kinematic chain constitutes the backbone of the robotic structure, where each link represents a rigid arm segment and each joint represents an actuator-driven degree of freedom. The chain is said to be **closed** if every link is connected to at least two others forming a loop, and **open** if the links form a serial path from a fixed base to the end-effector with no closed loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** A kinematic chain is the *ordered topological graph* of links and joints. One link must be **fixed** (the ground/frame) for the chain to be a **mechanism**. A chain without a fixed link is called a **kinematic chain** in the abstract sense; once grounded, it becomes a **mechanism**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Think of a kinematic chain exactly like your **human arm**:

- **Humerus (upper arm bone)** → Link 1
- **Elbow joint** → Revolute pair (R)
- **Radius/Ulna (forearm)** → Link 2
- **Wrist joint** → Spherical pair (S)
- **Palm/Fingers** → End-effector (TCP - Tool Center Point)

Just as your shoulder, elbow, and wrist allow your hand to reach a glass of water in 3D space through coordinated angular motion, an industrial manipulator's kinematic chain allows its end-effector to reach any point within its **workspace** through coordinated joint motion. If you lock your shoulder to the ground (say, by bolting it to a wall) — that's exactly what happens when a manipulator's base is fixed to the factory floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Machine** = Frame + Mechanism (does useful work)
> - **Mechanism** = Fixed link + Kinematic Chain (transmits motion)
> - **Structure** = Fixed link + Kinematic Chain with **zero DOF** (carries load only)

## 1.3 Standard Nomenclature in Kinematic Chains

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Link | $n$ | Rigid body in the chain |
| Joint (Pair) | $j$ | Constrained connection between two links |
| Degree of Freedom (DOF) | $M$ or $F$ | Minimum independent parameters to define configuration |
| Fixed Link (Frame) | 1 | The grounded reference link |
| End-Effector | TCP | The working end of the chain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The **mobility** (degrees of freedom) of a kinematic chain determines whether it is a **structure** ($F=0$), a **mechanism** ($F=1$), or a **multiple DOF system** ($F \geq 2$). For a 6-DOF industrial manipulator, exactly **6 independent actuators** are required to fully control the end-effector's pose in 3D space.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Pairs (Joints)

A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two links that allows specific relative motion while restricting others. The two broad classes are:

### 2.1.1 Lower Pairs (Surface / Area Contact)
These joints have **conforming surface contact**, meaning the contacting surfaces match each other geometrically. They can transmit heavy loads and are **wear-resistant**.

| Pair Type | Symbol | DOF | Constrained DOF | Example in Manipulator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (Hinge) | R | 1 | 5 | Elbow, shoulder, wrist joints |
| Prismatic (Sliding) | P | 1 | 5 | Linear actuator in Cartesian robot |
| Cylindrical | C | 2 | 4 | Screw-on lead (rare in manipulators) |
| Spherical (Ball) | S | 3 | 3 | Ball-and-socket wrist |
| Planar (E) | E | 3 | 3 | Slider on flat plate |
| Screw (Helical) | H | 1 | 5 | Lead screw drive |

### 2.1.2 Higher Pairs (Point / Line Contact)
Contact is at a **point or along a line**. Less wear-resistant, suited for high-speed motion with low load.

| Pair Type | DOF | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Cam-Follower | 2 | Internal combustion valve |
| Gear Pair | 2 | Reduction gearbox at joint |
| Rolling Contact | 2-3 | Wheel on road |

## 2.2 Classification of Kinematic Chains (Topology)

### 2.2.1 Based on Closure
- **Open Chain (Serial):** Links form a single path from base to end-effector. Example: Articulated robot arm (PUMA, KUKA KR series).
- **Closed Chain (Parallel):** At least one loop exists. Example: Delta robot, Stewart platform.

### 2.2.2 Based on Motion Domain
- **Planar Chain:** All links move in parallel planes. Example: 4-bar linkage, slider-crank.
- **Spatial Chain:** Links move in 3D space. Example: Industrial manipulator arm.

### 2.2.3 Based on Complexity
- **Simple Chain:** No redundant paths.
- **Compound Chain:** Multiple simple chains share common links.
- **Complex Chain:** No two links form more than one pair; no simple or compound reduction exists.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Mobility / DOF Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most heavily tested formulas in Module 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus.**

### Kutzbach Criterion (Planar Mechanisms)

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Where:
- $M$ = degrees of freedom (mobility)
- $n$ = total number of links (including the fixed link/frame)
- $j_1$ = number of **lower pairs** (1 DOF each)
- $j_2$ = number of **higher pairs** (2 DOF each)

### Kutzbach Criterion (Spatial Mechanisms — 3D)

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where $j_1, j_2, j_3, j_4, j_5$ represent pairs with $1, 2, 3, 4,$ and $5$ degrees of freedom respectively.

### Grübler's Special Form (for Manipulators)

$$M = 6n - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)$$

Where $f_i$ is the DOF of the $i$-th joint.

### Loop Closure Equation (for Closed Chains)

$$\sum_{i=1}^{p} \vec{f}_{i} = 0$$

Where $\vec{f}_{i}$ is the vector along the $i$-th link in a closed loop of $p$ links.

### Workspace Volume (Spherical Revolute Arm)

$$V_{workspace} = \frac{4}{3} \pi L_{\max}^3$$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

| Application | Use of Kinematic Chain Theory |
|---|---|
| Industrial Robot Design | Determines actuator count and joint type (R, P) |
| Automotive Suspension | Closed-chain 4-bar for wheel control |
| Aircraft Landing Gear | Multi-loop kinematic chain for retraction |
| Surgical Robots | Parallel chains for precision (Da Vinci system) |
| CNC Machines | Serial chain of P and R joints for tool positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Insight:** A 6-DOF PUMA robot has 6 revolute joints (RRRRRR). The well-known **Stanford Arm** is RPRRRR. The **SCARA** robot is RPRP. Each is designed for a specific application — knowing DOF theory lets an engineer select the **minimum DOF** for the task.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kutzbach Planar Mobility Equation

We will derive the formula $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ from first principles using the **principle of constrained motion in 2D space**.

### Step 1 — Degrees of Freedom of a Free Rigid Body in 2D

A single rigid body (link) moving freely in a plane has **3 independent parameters**:
- Translation along x-axis: $x$
- Translation along y-axis: $y$
- Rotation about z-axis: $\theta$

Therefore, the free DOF per link in 2D is exactly **3**.

### Step 2 — Total DOF if No Joints Existed

If we have $n$ unconnected links in 2D space, total free DOF is:

$$F_{free} = 3n$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the Fixed Link's Constraint

One link (the **ground / frame**) is fixed. Fixing a link in 2D removes its **3 DOF**:

$$F_{free, \text{grounded}} = 3n - 3 = 3(n - 1)$$

### Step 4 — Subtract Constraints Introduced by Each Joint

Each **lower pair** (1 DOF joint) in 2D removes exactly $3 - 1 = 2$ DOF.
Each **higher pair** (2 DOF joint) in 2D removes exactly $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF.

So total constraints removed by joints:

$$F_{constrained} = 2j_1 + 1 \cdot j_2$$

### Step 5 — Final Mobility

Combining Steps 3 and 4:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

This is the **Kutzbach Planar Mobility Equation** for any planar kinematic chain.

### Step 6 — Extension to Spatial (3D) Mechanisms

In 3D space, a free rigid body has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). A lower pair with $f$ DOF removes $6 - f$ constraints. Summing over all $n-1$ moving links and all $j$ joints:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)$$

Expanding for pairs classified by DOF:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

This is the **Spatial Kutzbach Equation** (Kutzbach-Grübler criterion).

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Slider-Crank Mechanism (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** A slider-crank mechanism consists of a crank, connecting rod, slider, and a fixed cylinder. Determine the mobility using Kutzbach's equation.

**Given:**
- Number of links, $n = 4$ (crank, connecting rod, slider, frame)
- Lower pairs: crank-frame (R), connecting rod-crank (R), connecting rod-slider (R), slider-frame (P) → $j_1 = 4$
- Higher pairs: $j_2 = 0$

**Solution:**

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

Substituting:

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0$$

$$M = 3(3) - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Result:** $M = 1$. The slider-crank is a **1-DOF mechanism** — one input (crank rotation) produces one output (slider translation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $M = 1$, the mechanism is **deterministic and fully controllable** with a single actuator (the crank motor). This is the foundational analysis for engine kinematics, a favourite KTU exam topic.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Four-Bar Linkage Mobility Check

**Problem:** A planar four-bar linkage has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Verify whether it is a mechanism, structure, or multi-DOF system.

**Given:**
- $n = 4$
- $j_1 = 4$ (4 revolute pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$

**Solution:**

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0$$

$$M = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Result:** $M = 1$, confirming it is a **mechanism** with 1 DOF. A single input rotation (say, the shortest link) drives a fully predictable, continuous coupler motion.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — 6-DOF Spatial Manipulator (RRRRRR)

**Problem:** Verify the mobility of a 6-revolute-joint spatial industrial manipulator (e.g., PUMA arm).

**Given:**
- $n = 7$ (6 links + 1 ground frame)
- $j_1 = 6$ (6 revolute joints, each with 1 DOF)
- $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

**Solution (Spatial Kutzbach):**

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

$$M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0$$

$$M = 36 - 30 = 6$$

**Result:** $M = 6$ ✓. The 6-DOF manipulator can position and orient its end-effector with **6 independent actuator inputs**, exactly matching the 3 translations and 3 rotations in 3D space.

## 3.5 Special Case — Gruebler's Paradox (Identical Links in Redundant Chain)

When a chain has **redundant DOF** (e.g., two parallel paths between links), the Kutzbach formula gives a *lower bound* rather than the true mobility. Such chains are called **Gruebler-equivalent** and require deeper analysis using **screw theory** or **Chebychev-Grübler-Kutzbach** with constraint equations:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + \text{(special passive DOF terms)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often apply the planar Kutzbach formula to a 3D spatial chain. Always check the problem's geometry first. A chain operating in 3D space requires the **6-DOF** version of the equation.

## 3.6 Inverse Kinematics — Loop Closure Method (Closed Chain)

For a closed kinematic chain with $p$ links, the position vector must sum to zero:

$$\vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 + \cdots + \vec{r}_p = 0$$

For a 4-bar linkage with link lengths $L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4$ and angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$:

$$L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 - L_3 \cos\theta_3 - L_4 \cos\theta_4 = 0$$

$$L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin\theta_2 - L_3 \sin\theta_3 - L_4 \sin\theta_4 = 0$$

These two equations are the **Freudenstein equations** when properly rearranged, and they form the foundation of closed-chain manipulator kinematics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Kinematic Chain Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Kinematic Chain System] --> B[Open Chain]
    A --> C[Closed Chain]
    A --> D[Compound Chain]
    A --> E[Complex Chain]
    B --> F[Serial Manipulator]
    B --> G[Cartesian Robot XYZ]
    C --> H[Parallel Manipulator]
    C --> I[Delta Robot]
    C --> J[Stewart Platform]
    D --> K[Multi-loop Industrial Robot]
    E --> L[Epicyclic Gearbox]
    F --> M[KUKA KR Series]
    F --> N[PUMA Arm]
    H --> O[Hexapod Motion Platform]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Degrees of Freedom Determination Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Step 1: Identify Problem Geometry] --> Q{Planar or Spatial?}
    Q --> R[Planar]
    Q --> S[Spatial]
    R --> T[Step 2: Count Links n]
    S --> U[Step 2: Count Links n]
    T --> V[Step 3: Count Lower Pairs j1]
    U --> W[Step 3: Count Joints by DOF]
    V --> X[Step 4: Count Higher Pairs j2]
    W --> Y[Step 4: Classify j1 to j5]
    X --> Z[Step 5: Apply M equals 3n minus 1 minus 2j1 minus j2]
    Y --> AA[Step 5: Apply M equals 6n minus 1 minus 5j1 minus 4j2 minus 3j3 minus 2j4 minus j5]
    Z --> AB[Step 6: Interpret M]
    AA --> AB
    AB --> AC{M equals 0?}
    AB --> AD{M equals 1?}
    AB --> AE{M greater than 1?}
    AC --> AF[Structure]
    AD --> AG[Mechanism]
    AE --> AH[Multi DOF System]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — 4-Bar Kinematic Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ground Frame Link 1] -->|Revolute Pair A| B[Input Crank Link 2]
    B -->|Revolute Pair B| C[Coupler Link 3]
    C -->|Revolute Pair C| D[Output Rocker Link 4]
    D -->|Revolute Pair D| A
    C --> E[Coupler Point Trace]
```

## 4.4 Block Functional Architecture — 6-DOF Industrial Manipulator

```mermaid
graph TD
    Base[Base Link L0] --> J1[Revolute Joint J1 Shoulder Yaw]
    J1 --> L1[Link L1 Upper Arm]
    L1 --> J2[Revolute Joint J2 Shoulder Pitch]
    J2 --> L2[Link L2 Forearm]
    L2 --> J3[Revolute Joint J3 Elbow]
    J3 --> L3[Link L3 Wrist 1]
    L3 --> J4[Revolute Joint J4 Wrist Roll]
    J4 --> L4[Link L4 Wrist 2]
    L4 --> J5[Revolute Joint J5 Wrist Pitch]
    J5 --> L5[Link L5 Wrist 3]
    L5 --> J6[Revolute Joint J6 Tool Roll]
    J6 --> TCP[Tool Center Point End Effector]
    Controller[Central Motion Controller] -->|Actuator Signal| J1
    Controller -->|Actuator Signal| J2
    Controller -->|Actuator Signal| J3
    Controller -->|Actuator Signal| J4
    Controller -->|Actuator Signal| J5
    Controller -->|Actuator Signal| J6
    Encoders[Joint Encoders] -->|Feedback| Controller
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the functional block architecture of a standard 6-DOF articulated manipulator (RRRRRR configuration). Notice that the **controller**, **actuators** (not shown), and **encoders** form a closed feedback loop. The kinematic chain itself is the *physical* (open) chain from Base to TCP. The mobility $M = 6$ matches the number of revolute joints, satisfying the spatial Kutzbach criterion.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define a kinematic chain. Differentiate between a kinematic chain, a mechanism, and a structure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kinematic chain** is a series of rigid links connected by kinematic pairs (joints) such that each link can move relative to its neighbours. A **mechanism** is obtained when one link of a kinematic chain is **fixed to the ground**, allowing it to transmit motion and force. A **structure** is a kinematic chain with a fixed link whose **mobility $M = 0$** — it carries loads but cannot move. In contrast, a mechanism has $M \geq 1$ and produces purposeful motion. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*State the Kutzbach criterion for a planar mechanism. What does a mobility of zero signify?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kutzbach criterion for a planar mechanism is:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where $n$ is the total number of links (including frame), $j_1$ is the number of lower pairs, and $j_2$ is the number of higher pairs. A mobility of **zero ($M = 0$)** signifies a **structure** — the chain cannot move relative to its fixed link; it is statically determinate and acts as a load-bearing framework. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Classify kinematic pairs in detail. Explain with neat sketches the six types of lower pairs and indicate their relative motion. (7 marks)

**(b)** A four-bar kinematic chain has links of length 100 mm, 200 mm, 250 mm, and 300 mm. Using Kutzbach's criterion, verify the mobility. If the shortest link is fixed, name the mechanism so obtained. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Kinematic Pairs (7 marks):**

Kinematic pairs are classified based on:
1. **Nature of contact:** Lower pair (surface contact) vs Higher pair (point/line contact).
2. **Type of relative motion:** Sliding, rolling, turning, or screw.
3. **Type of closure:** Self-closed (gravity/spring) vs Force-closed (external).

**The six lower pairs are:**

| # | Pair | Symbol | DOF | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Revolute (Hinge) | R | 1 | Pure rotation, e.g., robot elbow |
| 2 | Prismatic (Slider) | P | 1 | Pure translation, e.g., linear stage |
| 3 | Cylindrical | C | 2 | Rotation + translation, e.g., screw-sleeve |
| 4 | Spherical (Ball) | S | 3 | 3 rotations, no translation, e.g., hip joint |
| 5 | Planar (Slab) | E | 3 | 2 translations + 1 rotation in plane |
| 6 | Screw (Helical) | H | 1 | Helical motion (e.g., lead screw) |

(7 marks — 1 mark definition, 4 marks table, 2 marks applications)

**(b) Mobility Verification of 4-Bar (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n = 4$ (including fixed frame), $j_1 = 4$ (4 revolute pairs), $j_2 = 0$.

**Applying Kutzbach:**

$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1$$

**Result:** $M = 1$ — a **single-DOF mechanism**. Since the shortest link (100 mm) is **fixed** (grounded), this becomes a **crank-rocker mechanism** (because $L_{short} + L_{longest} < L_{other1} + L_{other2}$ → Grashof's condition: $100 + 300 = 400 < 200 + 250 = 450$, Grashof's law satisfied with shortest as crank). The shortest link makes a **full rotation** (crank), and the opposite link rocks (oscillates). (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of the Kutzbach criterion, derive the equation for the mobility of a spatial kinematic chain having $n$ links and $j$ joints. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 6-DOF industrial manipulator has the joint configuration R-P-R-R-R-R (Stanford arm type). Compute its mobility using the spatial Kutzbach criterion and state what the result implies. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Spatial Kutzbach Criterion (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** A free rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** — three translations $(x, y, z)$ and three rotations $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$.

**Step 2:** For $n$ unconnected links, free DOF $= 6n$.

**Step 3:** Fix one link to ground: subtract 6 DOF.

$$F_{remaining} = 6(n - 1)$$

**Step 4:** A joint with $f_i$ degrees of freedom removes $6 - f_i$ constraints. For all joints:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)$$

**Step 5:** Final mobility:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)$$

**Step 6:** For pairs classified as $j_1$ (1-DOF), $j_2$ (2-DOF), $j_3$ (3-DOF), $j_4$ (4-DOF), $j_5$ (5-DOF):

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

(7 marks — 1 mark step 1, 2 marks step 2-3, 2 marks step 4-5, 2 marks step 6 final form)

**(b) Mobility of Stanford Arm (7 marks):**

**Given:** R-P-R-R-R-R configuration.
- Total links $n = 7$ (6 moving + 1 frame)
- Joints: 1 P (prismatic, 1 DOF), 5 R (revolute, 1 DOF) — all are 1-DOF lower pairs
- $j_1 = 6$ (revolute) + $1$ (prismatic) = $7$? Wait — let me recount.

**Correct enumeration:** R-P-R-R-R-R → 6 joints, all 1-DOF. So $j_1 = 6$, $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

**Applying Spatial Kutzbach:**

$$M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 0$$

$$M = 36 - 30 = 6$$

**Implication:** $M = 6$ confirms the manipulator has **full 6 DOF** — it can independently control **3 positions + 3 orientations** of the end-effector, matching the natural degrees of freedom of a rigid body in 3D space. **6 actuators are mandatory** for full task-space control. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the fixed link:** Always include the **ground/frame** when counting $n$. Most students miss this and end up with wrong answers.
> 2. **Mixing planar and spatial equations:** A 3D chain *must* use the 6-DOF Kutzbach formula. Using the 3-DOF (planar) form on a 3D problem loses 5-7 marks.
> 3. **Misclassifying joint DOF:** A revolute joint has 1 DOF; a spherical joint has 3 DOF. Mistaking spherical for revolute is a frequent error.
> 4. **Not stating units or interpretation:** Always end the solution with a sentence interpreting $M$ (mechanism / structure / multi-DOF).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Kinematic Chains**

- A **kinematic chain** is a system of rigid links connected by joints; with one link fixed, it becomes a **mechanism**.
- **Lower pairs** have surface contact (6 types: R, P, C, S, E, H); **higher pairs** have point/line contact.
- **Kutzbach Planar Equation:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- **Kutzbach Spatial Equation:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- **Open chain** = serial; **closed chain** = parallel (with loops).
- $M = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Structure**; $M = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Mechanism**; $M \geq 2 \Rightarrow$ **Multi-DOF system**.
- Always count the **ground frame** as one of the $n$ links.
- **Grashof's Law** (for 4-bar): $S + L \leq P + Q$ for full continuous rotation of shortest link.
- **Industrial manipulator standard DOF** = **6** (3 position + 3 orientation).
- **6-DOF RRRRRR arm** mobility verification: $M = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 6$ ✓
- **Closed chain constraint** (loop closure): $\sum \vec{r}_i = 0$ — must always hold.
- **Workspace** of a revolute arm (spherical reach): $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi L_{max}^3$
- **DOF** is also called **mobility** or **degree of freedom** — all three terms are interchangeable in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Denavit Hartenberg Representation - Kinematics of Manipulator Structures - Three Link Planar Arm - Parallelogram Arm - Anthropomorphic Arm - Spherical Arm
