- **What IJC ignores** (state explicitly in derivations): configuration-dependent inertia $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, Coriolis/centrifugal $\mathbf{C}$, gravity $\mathbf{G}$. This is the *limitation* you mention if asked "Why is IJC not used for high-speed manipulators?". ✅
- **Common KTU numerical parameters:** $J = 1, 2, 5 \text{ kg·m}^2$, $B = 2, 4, 10 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$, $\omega_n = 5, 10, 20 \text{ rad/s}$, $\zeta = 0.7, 0.8, 1.0$. ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT631 - INDUSTRIAL MANIPULATORS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Feedback Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Feedback Control** in the context of industrial manipulators refers to a closed-loop control strategy in which the actual output (joint position, velocity, or torque) of a robotic manipulator is continuously measured by sensors and compared against a desired reference input. The resulting error signal is processed by a controller to generate corrective actuator commands, thereby driving the system output toward the desired trajectory with high precision.

Mathematically, the fundamental feedback control law is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_{c} \cdot e(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the control signal sent to the actuator, $K_{c}$ is the controller gain, and $e(t) = q_{d}(t) - q(t)$ is the tracking error between the desired joint position $q_{d}(t)$ and the measured actual joint position $q(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The feedback control loop is the *backbone* of every industrial manipulator. Without it, a robot cannot compensate for payload variations, friction, external disturbances, or modeling uncertainties. KTU questions frequently test the difference between **open-loop** and **closed-loop** architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car toward a parking spot:

- **Open-Loop (No Feedback):** You close your eyes, turn the steering wheel by a *fixed* amount, and press the accelerator for a *fixed* time, hoping the car lands in the spot. Any gust of wind, slope, or obstacle will ruin the maneuver.
- **Closed-Loop (Feedback):** You keep your eyes open, *continuously compare* the car's actual position to the desired spot, and constantly adjust the steering and accelerator. The smaller the gap between your car and the target, the smaller the correction you make.

> [!NOTE]
> In a manipulator, your **eyes** are the *encoders/resolvers*, your **brain** is the *controller*, and your **hands/feet** are the *motors/actuators*. The robot does exactly the same thing — thousands of times per second.

### 1.3 Why Feedback Control is Mandatory for Manipulators

- **Payload Uncertainty:** A robot may handle a 1 kg part one moment and a 50 kg part the next. Feedback automatically adapts the joint torques.
- **Nonlinear Dynamics:** Coriolis, centripetal, and gravitational terms in the manipulator equation $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ are highly coupled and configuration-dependent.
- **External Disturbances:** Contact forces, vibrations, and environmental noise must be rejected.
- **Modeling Errors:** Real robots never match their mathematical models perfectly.

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Desired position | $q_{d}(t)$ | **rad** or **m** | Reference trajectory |
| Actual position | $q(t)$ | **rad** or **m** | Measured by encoder |
| Tracking error | $e(t)$ | **rad** or **m** | $e = q_{d} - q$ |
| Control signal | $u(t)$ | **V** or **Nm** | Sent to actuator |
| Sampling rate | $f_{s}$ | **Hz** | Typically **1 kHz** in industrial robots |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Manipulator Feedback Loop

A standard single-joint feedback control system consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Reference Input ($R(s)$):** The desired joint trajectory, generated by the trajectory planner.
2. **Comparator (Summing Junction):** Computes the error $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$.
3. **Controller ($C(s)$):** Processes the error and produces the control effort. May be P, PI, PD, or PID.
4. **Actuator / Power Amplifier ($A(s)$):** Motor driver that converts low-level signal into high-power current/voltage.
5. **Plant / Manipulator Joint ($G(s)$):** The physical robotic joint, including motor, gear, and link inertia.
6. **Sensor ($H(s)$):** Encoder, resolver, or tachometer that measures the output.
7. **Feedback Path:** Returns $Y(s)$ to the comparator. Typically $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback) in manipulator joints.

### 2.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Transfer Functions

For a plant $G(s)$ and controller $C(s)$ with unity feedback:

**Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF):**

$$G_{OL}(s) = C(s) \cdot G(s)$$

**Closed-Loop Transfer Function (CLTF):**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{C(s) \cdot G(s)}{1 + C(s) \cdot G(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The expression $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ is the **Characteristic Equation** of the closed-loop system. Its roots determine the stability, response speed, and oscillation behaviour.

### 2.3 Standard PID Controller Structure

The most widely used controller in industrial manipulators is the **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** controller. Its continuous-time output is:

$$u(t) = K_{p} e(t) + K_{i} \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_{d} \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

In the Laplace domain (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$U(s) = \left( K_{p} + \frac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d} s \right) E(s)$$

| Controller Type | Transfer Function $C(s)$ | Effect on System |
|---|---|---|
| **P** (Proportional) | $K_{p}$ | Reduces rise time, but never eliminates steady-state error |
| **I** (Integral) | $\dfrac{K_{i}}{s}$ | Eliminates steady-state error, may cause oscillation |
| **D** (Derivative) | $K_{d} s$ | Improves damping, reduces overshoot |
| **PD** | $K_{p} + K_{d} s$ | Better transient response, no steady-state error elimination |
| **PID** | $K_{p} + \dfrac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d} s$ | Combines all three benefits — the workhorse of robotics |

### 2.4 Steady-State Error

The **steady-state error** $e_{ss}$ quantifies the persistent gap between desired and actual output after transients have died out. For a unity-feedback system driven by an input of the form $R(s) = \dfrac{A}{s^{n}}$ (step, ramp, parabola), the steady-state error is computed using the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

The relevant constants are:

$$K_{p}^{\text{pos}} = \lim_{s \to 0} C(s)G(s) \quad (\text{Position Constant})$$

$$K_{v} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot C(s)G(s) \quad (\text{Velocity Constant})$$

$$K_{a} = \lim_{s \to 0} s^{2} \cdot C(s)G(s) \quad (\text{Acceleration Constant})$$

### 2.5 Stability Criterion — Routh-Hurwitz

A closed-loop system is **stable** if and only if all roots of the characteristic polynomial $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ lie in the **left-half of the s-plane** (negative real parts). The Routh-Hurwitz criterion provides a necessary and sufficient algebraic condition without solving for the roots.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Application in Manipulators |
|---|---|---|
| Tracking Error | $e(t) = q_{d}(t) - q(t)$ | Core input to every controller |
| Error in s-domain | $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ | Used in block-diagram algebra |
| Closed-Loop TF | $\dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | Joint position servo design |
| Characteristic Eqn. | $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ | Stability analysis |
| PID Output (time) | $K_{p} e + K_{i}\!\int e \,dt + K_{d}\dot{e}$ | Motor torque/command |
| PID Output (Laplace) | $\left(K_{p} + \dfrac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d}s\right)E(s)$ | Frequency-domain design |
| Steady-state error | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} \frac{sR(s)}{1+C(s)G(s)}$ | Trajectory tracking accuracy |
| Position constant $K_{p}$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} C(s)G(s)$ | Step-input error |
| Velocity constant $K_{v}$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} sC(s)G(s)$ | Ramp-input error |
| Acceleration constant $K_{a}$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} s^{2}C(s)G(s)$ | Parabolic-input error |
| Routh array first column | All positive $\Rightarrow$ Stable | Quick stability test |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

Feedback control is what makes industrial manipulators commercially viable. It enables:

- **Welding robots** to maintain sub-millimeter seam tracking.
- **SCARA pick-and-place** arms to achieve cycle times under 100 ms.
- **Surgical robots (e.g., da Vinci)** to filter surgeon hand-tremors through high-gain feedback.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots)** to instantly stop upon human contact via force-feedback.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function

**Given:** A unity-feedback system with forward path $C(s)G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Write the error in the s-domain.**
The comparator subtracts the feedback signal from the reference:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$$

**Step 2 — Express the control effort.**
The controller and plant process the error sequentially:

$$U(s) = C(s) \cdot E(s)$$

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s)$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two forward-path equations.**
Substituting $E(s)$:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot C(s) \cdot [R(s) - Y(s)]$$

**Step 4 — Expand and collect terms.**
$$Y(s) = C(s)G(s) \cdot R(s) - C(s)G(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

$$Y(s) + C(s)G(s) \cdot Y(s) = C(s)G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$Y(s) \cdot [1 + C(s)G(s)] = C(s)G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $Y(s)/R(s)$.**

$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}}$$

This is the canonical **closed-loop transfer function** of a unity-feedback manipulator joint.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Error for a Step Input

**Given:** Reference $R(s) = \dfrac{A}{s}$ (step of magnitude $A$), unity feedback.

**Step 1 — Write the error equation.**
From the block diagram, $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$. Using the CLTF derived above:

$$Y(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} \cdot R(s)$$

$$E(s) = R(s) - \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} R(s)$$

$$E(s) = R(s) \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the bracketed term.**

$$E(s) = R(s) \cdot \frac{1 + C(s)G(s) - C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \frac{R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R(s) = A/s$.**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (A/s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{A}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Position Error Constant $K_{p}^{\text{pos}}$.**

$$K_{p}^{\text{pos}} = \lim_{s \to 0} C(s)G(s)$$

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1 + K_{p}^{\text{pos}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the open-loop system is **Type 1 or higher** (i.e., it contains an integrator, like a PI controller), then $K_{p}^{\text{pos}} \to \infty$ and $e_{ss} \to 0$ for a step input. This is precisely why integral action is added to manipulator joint controllers.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stability & Performance Trade-off

| Controller Type | Steady-State Error (Step) | Overshoot | Rise Time | Settling Time | Suitable for Manipulator Joint? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P (low $K_{p}$) | Large | Low | Slow | Slow | Poor — only for crude positioning |
| P (high $K_{p}$) | Small | High | Fast | Slow (oscillates) | Risky — actuator saturation |
| PD | Non-zero | Low | Fast | Fast | Good for trajectory tracking |
| PI | **Zero** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent for setpoint regulation |
| **PID** | **Zero** | Tunable | Fast | Fast | **Industry standard for joints** |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — PD Controller on a Single Joint

**Plant:** A single robotic joint modeled as an inertia $J$ with viscous friction $b$. The transfer function from torque $\tau$ to position $q$ is:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{s(Js + b)}$$

**Controller:** $C(s) = K_{p} + K_{d}s$.

**Step 1 — Form the open-loop transfer function.**

$$C(s)G(s) = \frac{K_{p} + K_{d}s}{s(Js + b)}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function.**

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{K_{p} + K_{d}s}{s(Js + b) + K_{p} + K_{d}s}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the denominator (characteristic polynomial).**

$$s(Js + b) + K_{p} + K_{d}s = Js^{2} + bs + K_{p} + K_{d}s = Js^{2} + (b + K_{d})s + K_{p}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the standard second-order form.**
Comparing with $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}$:

$$\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{K_{p}}{J}}, \quad 2\zeta\omega_{n} = \frac{b + K_{d}}{J}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the damping ratio.**

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{b + K_{d}}{2\sqrt{J \cdot K_{p}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Increasing $K_{d}$ directly increases damping — this is why derivative action is critical for manipulator joints, which are inherently low-damped second-order systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Manipulator Joint Feedback Loop

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *complete* signal flow inside one manipulator joint, from the trajectory planner down to the physical motor. It emphasizes both the forward (control) path and the feedback (measurement) path, as well as the disturbance injection point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Trajectory q_d_t] --> B[Comparator Subtract]
    M[Measured Joint Position q_t] --> B
    B --> C[Controller P PI PD PID]
    C --> D[Power Amplifier / Motor Driver]
    D --> E[Manipulator Joint Plant M_q C_q G_q]
    E --> F[Physical Output Joint Motion]
    E --> G[Encoder / Resolver Sensor]
    G --> H[Signal Conditioning and Digitization]
    H --> M
    N[External Disturbance Load Variation Friction] --> E
    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
    style N fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Block Diagram in Laplace Domain

This second diagram uses standard control-system notation ($R, E, U, Y$) so it directly maps to the transfer-function derivations in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R_s Reference Input] --> SUM((Sum))
    SUM --> E[E_s Error]
    E --> C[C_s Controller]
    C --> A[A_s Actuator]
    A --> P[G_s Plant Manipulator Joint]
    P --> Y[Y_s Output]
    Y --> FB[Feedback Path H_s]
    FB --> SUM
    style R fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#333
    style Y fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333
    style SUM fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 PID Controller Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Error Signal e_t] --> P[Proportional Kp]
    E --> I[Integral Ki Integral of e]
    E --> D[Derivative Kd times de/dt]
    P --> SUM2((Add))
    I --> SUM2
    D --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> U[Control Signal u_t]
    style U fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#333
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide for Students:**
> - In **Diagram 4.1**, trace the loop *clockwise*: the trajectory is commanded, the error is computed using the *measured* (feedback) signal, the controller decides what torque to apply, and the actuator drives the joint. The encoder closes the loop.
> - In **Diagram 4.2**, the same loop is shown in compact control-theory form. $H(s)$ is the sensor dynamics; for most encoders, $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback).
> - **Diagram 4.3** reveals the three parallel branches of a PID controller. Each branch processes the *same* error signal but in a different way (instantaneous, accumulated, rate of change), and their outputs are summed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **feedback control** in the context of industrial manipulators. List two advantages of closed-loop control over open-loop control.

**Model Answer:**

Feedback control is a control strategy in which the actual output (joint position, velocity, or torque) of a manipulator is measured by a sensor and continuously compared with the desired reference to generate an error signal. This error drives a controller that commands the actuator to minimize the difference.

**Advantages over open-loop control:**
1. It automatically corrects for **disturbances** such as varying payloads and external forces.
2. It compensates for **modeling uncertainties** and parameter variations (e.g., friction changes with temperature).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the role of the **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** controller in a robotic manipulator joint. What is the effect of increasing the derivative gain $K_{d}$?

**Model Answer:**

A PID controller processes the joint tracking error and produces a control torque that combines three actions: the **P-term** provides an immediate corrective effort proportional to the current error, the **I-term** eliminates steady-state error by accumulating past errors, and the **D-term** damps the response by reacting to the rate of change of error.

**Effect of increasing $K_{d}$:** It increases the **damping ratio** $\zeta$, which reduces overshoot and oscillation, improves transient stability, and leads to a smoother joint trajectory. Excessive $K_{d}$, however, amplifies sensor noise.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of a unity-feedback manipulator joint whose plant is $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$ and whose controller is a **PD controller** $C(s) = K_{p} + K_{d}s$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Form the open-loop transfer function.**
$$C(s)G(s) = \frac{10(K_{p} + K_{d}s)}{s(s+2)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the unity-feedback CLTF formula.**
$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \frac{10(K_{p} + K_{d}s)}{s(s+2) + 10(K_{p} + K_{d}s)}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the denominator.**
$$s^{2} + 2s + 10K_{p} + 10K_{d}s = s^{2} + (2 + 10K_{d})s + 10K_{p}$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-loop TF.**
$$\boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10(K_{p} + K_{d}s)}{s^{2} + (2 + 10K_{d})s + 10K_{p}}}$$

**(b)** For the system above, determine the values of $K_{p}$ and $K_{d}$ that yield a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$ and natural frequency $\omega_{n} = 5$ rad/s. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Standard second-order form.**
Comparing denominator with $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}$:
$$\omega_{n}^{2} = 10K_{p}, \quad 2\zeta\omega_{n} = 2 + 10K_{d}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $K_{p}$.**
$$K_{p} = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{10} = \frac{25}{10} = 2.5$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $K_{d}$.**
$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = 2(0.7)(5) = 7$$
$$7 = 2 + 10K_{d} \;\Rightarrow\; 10K_{d} = 5 \;\Rightarrow\; K_{d} = 0.5$$

$$\boxed{K_{p} = 2.5, \quad K_{d} = 0.5}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **components of a closed-loop feedback control system** for a single manipulator joint. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Narrative + Block Diagram):**

A single-joint manipulator feedback loop consists of the following blocks connected in a closed path:

1. **Reference Input $R(s)$:** The desired joint trajectory (e.g., $q_{d}(t) = \sin t$).
2. **Comparator / Summing Junction:** Computes $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$.
3. **Controller $C(s)$:** Usually a PID that shapes the error into a motor command.
4. **Power Amplifier / Motor Driver $A(s)$:** Scales the low-level signal to high current/voltage.
5. **Plant $G(s)$:** The joint dynamics — motor inertia, gear ratio, link mass.
6. **Sensor $H(s)$:** Encoder/resolver that measures $q(t)$.
7. **Feedback Path:** Returns the measured signal to the comparator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R Reference] --> S((Sum))
    S --> C[C Controller]
    C --> A[A Amplifier]
    A --> G[G Plant Joint]
    G --> Y[Y Output Position]
    Y --> H[H Sensor]
    H --> S
```

**Step-by-step signal flow:**
- $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s)$ — error computed.
- $U(s) = C(s)E(s)$ — controller output.
- $Y(s) = A(s)G(s)U(s)$ — actuator + plant produce motion.
- Sensor reads $Y(s)$ and feeds it back, completing the loop.

**(b)** Derive the **steady-state error** of this system for a **unit ramp input** $R(s) = 1/s^{2}$, assuming unity feedback and $C(s)G(s) = \dfrac{K(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the error expression.**
For unity feedback:
$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R(s) = 1/s^{2}$.**
$$E(s) = \frac{1/s^{2}}{1 + \dfrac{K(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+3)}} = \frac{(s+2)(s+3)}{s^{2}(s+2)(s+3) + K(s+1)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.**
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{(s+2)(s+3)}{s(s+2)(s+3) + K(s+1)/s}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $s = 0$.**
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{(2)(3)}{0 + K(1)} = \frac{6}{K}$$

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{6}{K}}$$

This equals $1/K_{v}$ where $K_{v}$ is the velocity error constant — a **Type-1** system has a finite ramp error.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in feedback-control questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unity-feedback assumption.** When the problem does not specify $H(s)$, the default in KTU exams is $H(s) = 1$. Writing $Y(s) = \dfrac{CG}{1+CGH}$ with $H = 1$ is mandatory.
> 2. **Skipping the Final Value Theorem step.** Steady-state error questions *always* require $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$, not just $E(s)$ at $s = 0$.
> 3. **Sign error in the comparator.** It is $E = R - Y$, not $Y - R$. This single sign mistake cascades through every transfer function.
> 4. **Confusing open-loop and closed-loop poles.** Stability is judged by the *closed-loop* characteristic equation $1 + CG = 0$, not by the open-loop poles of $G(s)$.
> 5. **Misapplying the Routh criterion.** The first column of the Routh array must be **strictly positive** for stability. A sign change or a zero in the first column requires special auxiliary-polynomial handling.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feedback control** = continuous measurement → error computation → controller action → actuator drive.
- The **closed-loop TF** is $\dfrac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ for unity feedback.
- The **characteristic equation** $1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$ governs stability.
- The **PID controller** $K_{p} + K_{i}/s + K_{d}s$ is the workhorse of manipulator joints: P reduces rise time, I eliminates steady-state error, D damps oscillations.
- **Steady-state error** is computed via $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ using the Final Value Theorem.
- **System Type** (number of free integrators in the open-loop) determines tracking ability: Type 0 → finite step error; Type 1 → zero step error, finite ramp error; Type 2 → zero step and ramp error.
- **Routh-Hurwitz** is the fastest algebraic test for closed-loop stability without solving the characteristic polynomial.
- For a single-joint plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(Js+b)}$, a **PD controller** is typically sufficient because the plant already provides one integrator.
- **Sampling rate** in modern industrial robots is typically **1 kHz**, well above the joint bandwidth (~10–100 Hz).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decentralized Feedforward Compensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT631 - INDUSTRIAL MANIPULATORS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decentralized Feedforward Compensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Decentralized Feedforward Compensation (DFC)** is a multi-input multi-output (MIMO) control architecture for robotic manipulators in which each joint is regulated by an **independent local feedback controller**, while the inherent inter-axis dynamic coupling effects (inertial, Coriolis/centrifugal, and gravitational) are cancelled *in advance* by an additive **feedforward signal** generated from a local (per-joint) dynamic model.

In KTU 2024-scheme terminology, it falls under the umbrella of *Independent Joint Control with Dynamic Decoupling Augmentation* — a hybrid that combines the **structural simplicity of decentralized (diagonal) control** with the **model-based accuracy of centralized computed-torque techniques**, but without requiring the *full* manipulator inertia matrix to be inverted in real time at a single global controller.

Mathematically, for an $n$-DOF manipulator governed by

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

the decentralized feedforward control law is

$$\tau = \underbrace{\tau_{ff}^{dec}}_{\text{feedforward}} + \underbrace{\tau_{fb}}_{\text{local feedback}}$$

where $\tau_{ff}^{dec}$ is computed from **only the diagonal (and possibly banded) terms** of the dynamic model, not the full matrix $M(q)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Decentralized feedforward compensation is presented as the practical engineering compromise between *purely decentralized* control (which ignores coupling, hence sluggish) and *fully centralized computed-torque control* (which requires full dynamic inversion, hence computationally heavy). It is the industry-preferred architecture for **6-DOF industrial arms** with real-time sampling rates of 1–4 kHz.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **row of eight oarsmen (rowers) in a long boat**:
- **Purely Decentralized Control** → each rower pulls at his own pace, eyes closed, ignoring the others. The boat weaves and wobbles because nobody compensates for the wake of his neighbour.
- **Fully Centralized Computed-Torque** → a single coxswain (the global controller) calculates *every* stroke of *every* oarsman from the boat's total equation of motion. Powerful, but the coxswain must shout complex instructions to all eight at once — slow and exhausting.
- **Decentralized Feedforward Compensation** → each oar feels a *small spring* (local PID feedback) that resists the rower's own errors, **plus** a *gentle pre-push* from the coxswain (the feedforward term) that says "expect this much disturbance from your neighbour at this instant". The oar still pulls *autonomously*, but is pre-loaded for the disturbance. **Fast, local, and still accurate.**

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

- **Tracking error norm:** typically bounded by $||e(t)|| \le \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \to 0$ as the feedforward model accuracy increases.
- **Cross-coupling rejection ratio:** feedforward compensates $C_{ij}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_j$ and $M_{ij}(q)\ddot{q}_j$ for $i \ne j$.
- **Computational load reduction:** the on-line inversion of an $n \times n$ matrix $M(q)$ is replaced by $n$ scalar divisions (diagonal elements), i.e. a reduction factor of $\mathcal{O}(n^2) \to \mathcal{O}(n)$.
- **Sampling latency budget:** industry standard $\le \mathbf{250\ \mu s}$ per joint cycle for a 6-DOF arm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental design invariant of DFC is:
> **"Decouple, but do not over-couple."** The feedforward must cancel *known* cross-coupling but the feedback must remain robust to *unknown* residual coupling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of the Manipulator Dynamic Equation

The standard **Lagrangian dynamic model** of an $n$-DOF serial-chain manipulator is

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;=\; \tau$$

where the symbols carry the following rigorous KTU 2024-scheme meaning:

| Symbol | Matrix/Vector Size | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | $n \times 1$ | Vector of joint displacements (rad or m) |
| $\dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ | $n \times 1$ | Joint velocity and acceleration vectors |
| $M(q)$ | $n \times n$ | Symmetric, positive-definite inertia matrix |
| $C(q,\dot{q})$ | $n \times n$ | Coriolis and centrifugal coefficient matrix |
| $G(q)$ | $n \times 1$ | Gravitational torque vector |
| $\tau$ | $n \times 1$ | Vector of actuator joint torques |

### 2.2 Structural Decomposition — The Heart of Decentralized Compensation

Expand the $i$-th row of the dynamic equation:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q}_j \;+\; G_i(q) \;=\; \tau_i$$

Separate the **diagonal (local)** term from the **off-diagonal (cross-coupling)** terms:

$$\underbrace{M_{ii}(q)\,\ddot{q}_i}_{\text{local inertia}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j}_{\text{cross inertia}} \;+\; \underbrace{C_{ii}(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q}_i}_{\text{local Coriolis}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{j \ne i} C_{ij}(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q}_j}_{\text{cross Coriolis}} \;+\; \underbrace{G_i(q)}_{\text{local gravity}} \;=\; \tau_i$$

**This single decomposition is the entire theoretical foundation of decentralized feedforward compensation.** The control law supplies:

- a **local feedback torque** $\tau_{fb,i}$ to handle $M_{ii}\ddot{q}_i + C_{ii}\dot{q}_i + G_i$ (the part that *would* have existed if the joint were isolated), and
- a **local feedforward torque** $\tau_{ff,i}^{dec}$ to cancel $\sum_{j \ne i}(M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + C_{ij}\dot{q}_j)$, the *known* coupling.

### 2.3 The Three-Layer Control Law

The complete DFC torque for joint $i$ is

$$\boxed{\;\tau_i \;=\; \underbrace{M_{ii}(q)\,v_i + \sum_{j\ne i}M_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}C_{ij}(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q}_j + G_i(q)}_{\tau_{ff,i}^{dec}} \;+\; \underbrace{K_{P,i}\,e_i + K_{D,i}\,\dot{e}_i + K_{I,i}\!\int e_i\,dt}_{\tau_{fb,i}}\;}$$

where

- $e_i = q_{d,i} - q_i$ is the position tracking error of joint $i$,
- $v_i = \ddot{q}_{d,i} + K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{P,i}e_i + K_{I,i}\!\int e_i dt$ is the so-called *auxiliary error dynamics* variable,
- $K_{P,i}, K_{D,i}, K_{I,i}$ are the diagonal PID gains (positive scalars).

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the **centralized computed-torque law** would set $v = \ddot{q}_d + K_D\dot{e}+K_Pe$ globally and compute $\tau = M(q)v + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$. The DFC version *replaces* the full matrix-vector product $M(q)v$ with a *scalar* $M_{ii}v_i$ and adds the cross-terms *one-by-one*. The result is a **banded or diagonal-dominant** computation.

### 2.4 Why the Diagonal-Dominant Trick Works

In most industrial manipulators, the inertia matrix is **diagonally dominant** at low speeds and at the *mid-range* of the workspace:

$$|M_{ii}(q)| \;\ge\; \sum_{j \ne i} |M_{ij}(q)| \quad \text{(row-sum dominance condition)}$$

When this holds, **Gershgorin's circle theorem** guarantees that all eigenvalues of $M(q)$ lie near the diagonal entries, so the diagonal-only feedback (without any cross-term compensation) is already *conditionally stable*. The feedforward term is then an *augmentation*, not a necessity.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Full Lagrangian dynamic model |
| 2 | $\tau_i = M_{ii}\ddot{q}_i + \sum_{j\ne i}M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_j C_{ij}\dot{q}_j + G_i$ | Row-wise decoupling of joint $i$ |
| 3 | $\tau = \tau_{ff}^{dec} + \tau_{fb}$ | DFC decomposition |
| 4 | $\tau_{ff,i}^{dec} = M_{ii}v_i + \sum_{j\ne i}M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_j C_{ij}\dot{q}_j + G_i$ | Decentralized feedforward law |
| 5 | $\tau_{fb,i} = K_{P,i}e_i + K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{I,i}\!\int e_i\,dt$ | Local PID feedback |
| 6 | $e_i = q_{d,i} - q_i$ | Tracking error definition |
| 7 | $v_i = \ddot{q}_{d,i} + K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{P,i}e_i + K_{I,i}\!\int e_i dt$ | Auxiliary error variable |
| 8 | $\dot{M} - 2C = $ skew-symmetric | Passivity property (used in Lyapunov proof) |
| 9 | $K_P, K_D \succ 0$ (PD) $\Rightarrow$ asymptotic stability of error | Lyapunov sufficient condition |
| 10 | Row-sum dominance: $\vert M_{ii}\vert \ge \sum_{j\ne i}\vert M_{ij}\vert$ | Condition enabling diagonal control |
| 11 | $T_{sample} \le 1\ \text{ms}$ (typ. 250 $\mu$s) | Real-time feasibility constraint |
| 12 | On-line FLOPS: $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ centralised vs $\mathcal{O}(n)$ DFC | Computational-complexity contrast |

> **Units used in the table:** $q$: rad (revolute) or m (prismatic); $\dot{q}$: rad/s or m/s; $\ddot{q}$: rad/s² or m/s²; $M_{ij}$: kg·m² or kg; $C_{ij}$: kg·m²/s or kg/s; $G_i$: N·m or N; $\tau_i$: N·m or N; $K_P$: N·m/rad or N/m; $K_D$: N·m·s/rad or N·s/m; $K_I$: N·m/(rad·s) or N/(m·s).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial painting & welding arms (e.g., KUKA KR 16, ABB IRB 6700):** every joint runs a decentralized servo loop at 1–4 kHz; DFC computes coupling torques at the slower 250 Hz trajectory-rate, *exactly* matching the DFC architecture.
- **Space manipulators (Canadarm, Dextre):** payload variations change $M(q)$ substantially — DFC re-uses an *estimated* $M_{ii}$ (and ignores the unknown $M_{ij}$ for $j \ne i$) since payload inertia is the dominant diagonal perturbation.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci):** the **safety-critical** nature of the application *forbids* full centralized control (single-point-of-failure); DFC's per-joint autonomy is inherently fault-tolerant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DFC Law from the Manipulator Dynamic Model

**Objective:** Starting from the full dynamic equation, derive the explicit decentralized feedforward compensation law with a local PID feedback.

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop objective.**
Define the desired joint trajectory as $q_d(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, with $\dot{q}_d$, $\ddot{q}_d$ bounded. The tracking error is

$$e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t), \quad \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}, \quad \ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}.$$

**Step 2 — Replace the actuator torque by a control law.**
We want a control torque $\tau$ such that $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. We postulate the control structure

$$\tau = M(q)\,v + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q), \quad \text{where } v = \ddot{q}_d + K_D\dot{e} + K_P e.$$

This is the **centralized computed-torque law**. If $M, C, G$ are known exactly, plugging in gives the linear closed-loop dynamics

$$\ddot{e} + K_D\dot{e} + K_P e = 0,$$

which is globally asymptotically stable for $K_P, K_D \succ 0$.

**Step 3 — Centralised law expanded row-wise.**
Writing $\tau = M v + C\dot{q} + G$ component-wise, the $i$-th torque is

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\,v_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}\,\dot{q}_j + G_i.$$

**Step 4 — Decompose into diagonal + off-diagonal parts.**

$$\tau_i = \underbrace{M_{ii}\,v_i}_{\text{diagonal}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij}\,v_j}_{\text{cross inertia FF}} + \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}\,\dot{q}_j}_{\text{Coriolis FF (all j)}} + \underbrace{G_i}_{\text{gravity FF}}.$$

**Step 5 — Identify the decentralised feedforward term.**
The first three groups together constitute $\tau_{ff,i}^{dec}$ — they are *computable* from the known model and the reference trajectory. Hence

$$\tau_{ff,i}^{dec} \;\triangleq\; M_{ii}\,v_i \;+\; \sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij}\,v_j \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}\,\dot{q}_j \;+\; G_i.$$

**Step 6 — Identify the local feedback term.**
Since the centralised law already produces a *linear* error equation $\ddot{e} + K_D\dot{e} + K_P e = 0$, we may equivalently add a **local** correction at each joint. Choose

$$\tau_{fb,i} = -K_{P,i}\,e_i - K_{D,i}\,\dot{e}_i \quad \text{(PD, decentralised)}.$$

**Step 7 — Combined DFC law.**

$$\boxed{\;\tau_i = \underbrace{M_{ii}\,v_i + \sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij}\,v_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}\,\dot{q}_j + G_i}_{\tau_{ff,i}^{dec}} \;+\; \underbrace{K_{P,i}\,e_i + K_{D,i}\,\dot{e}_i}_{\tau_{fb,i}}\;}$$

where $v_i = \ddot{q}_{d,i} + K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{P,i}e_i$ (the auxiliary variable *now* evaluated locally at joint $i$).

**Step 8 — Closed-loop error dynamics in the *nominal* case.**
If the model is exact and there is no model uncertainty, the closed-loop error equation is *identical* to the centralised case:

$$\ddot{e}_i + (K_{D,i}+K_{D,i}^{aux})\dot{e}_i + (K_{P,i}+K_{P,i}^{aux})\,e_i = 0,$$

which is a decoupled second-order linear system, stable for positive gains.

**Step 9 — Closed-loop error dynamics in the *real* (model-mismatched) case.**
Let $\tilde{M} = \hat{M} - M$ be the inertia estimation error, $\tilde{C}$, $\tilde{G}$ similarly defined. The actual closed-loop dynamics become

$$M(q)\ddot{e} + (C(q,\dot{q}) + K_D)\dot{e} + K_P e = \tilde{M}\,\ddot{q} + \tilde{C}\,\dot{q} + \tilde{G}.$$

**Step 10 — Boundedness of the tracking error (Lyapunov-style argument).**
Choose the Lyapunov candidate

$$V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q)\dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_P e.$$

Its time derivative is (using the skew-symmetry of $\dot M - 2C$)

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_D \dot{e} \le 0,$$

which proves **uniform stability**. With persistent excitation, the *bounded* residual error is

$$\|e\|_\infty \le \frac{\|M^{-1}\|\,\|\tilde{M}\ddot{q}+\tilde{C}\dot{q}+\tilde{G}\|}{\lambda_{\min}(K_P)} \quad \text{(steady-state bound)}.$$

> **Interpretation:** The decentralized feedforward compensation is **robust** in the sense that bounded model uncertainty produces bounded tracking error — *not* necessarily zero error, but with magnitude proportional to the uncertainty.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2-DOF Planar Arm

Consider a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link masses $m_1, m_2$, lengths $l_1, l_2$, and joint angles $q_1, q_2$. The inertia matrix and Coriolis matrix are

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 + 2a_2\cos q_2 & a_3 + a_2\cos q_2 \\ a_3 + a_2\cos q_2 & a_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C(q,\dot{q}) = a_2\sin q_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{q}_2 & -\dot{q}_1-\dot{q}_2 \\ \dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix},$$

where $a_1 = (m_1+m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + I_1 + I_2$, $a_2 = m_2 l_1 l_2$, $a_3 = m_2 l_2^2 + I_2$, and gravity is neglected ($G = 0$).

**Sample values:** $m_1 = 5$ kg, $m_2 = 3$ kg, $l_1 = 0.5$ m, $l_2 = 0.4$ m, $I_1 = 0.1$ kg·m², $I_2 = 0.05$ kg·m², $q_1 = 0.5$ rad, $q_2 = 0.3$ rad, $\dot{q}_1 = 0.2$ rad/s, $\dot{q}_2 = -0.1$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_{d,1} = 0.4$ rad/s², $\ddot{q}_{d,2} = 0.6$ rad/s², $K_{P,1} = K_{P,2} = 50$, $K_{D,1} = K_{D,2} = 10$.

**Numerical evaluation of the DFC torque for joint 1:**

First compute the parameters:
- $a_1 = (5+3)(0.5)^2 + 3(0.4)^2 + 0.1 + 0.05 = 2.0 + 0.48 + 0.15 = 2.63$
- $a_2 = 3 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.60$
- $a_3 = 3(0.4)^2 + 0.05 = 0.48 + 0.05 = 0.53$

Then evaluate $M_{11}$, $M_{12}$:
- $M_{11} = a_1 + 2 a_2 \cos q_2 = 2.63 + 2(0.60)\cos 0.3 = 2.63 + 1.2(0.9553) = 2.63 + 1.146 = 3.776$ kg·m²
- $M_{12} = a_3 + a_2 \cos q_2 = 0.53 + 0.60(0.9553) = 0.53 + 0.573 = 1.103$ kg·m²
- $C_{11} = -a_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_2 = -0.60 \sin 0.3 \times (-0.1) = 0.60(0.2955)(0.1) = 0.01773$
- $C_{12} = -a_2 \sin q_2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) = -0.60(0.2955)(0.2 - 0.1) = -0.01773$

Assuming zero tracking error for the FF term ($e_1 = e_2 = 0$ gives $v_1 = \ddot{q}_{d,1} = 0.4$, $v_2 = \ddot{q}_{d,2} = 0.6$):

$$\tau_{ff,1}^{dec} = M_{11} v_1 + M_{12} v_2 + C_{11} \dot{q}_1 + C_{12} \dot{q}_2 + 0$$
$$= 3.776(0.4) + 1.103(0.6) + 0.01773(0.2) + (-0.01773)(-0.1)$$
$$= 1.510 + 0.662 + 0.00355 + 0.00177 \approx 2.177\ \text{N·m}$$

**Numerical evaluation of the DFC torque for joint 2:**
- $M_{21} = M_{12} = 1.103$, $M_{22} = a_3 = 0.53$
- $C_{21} = a_2 \sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_1 = 0.60(0.2955)(0.2) = 0.03546$, $C_{22} = 0$

$$\tau_{ff,2}^{dec} = 0.53(0.6) + 1.103(0.4) + 0.03546(0.2) + 0$$
$$= 0.318 + 0.441 + 0.00709 \approx 0.766\ \text{N·m}$$

**Feedback contribution (with $e_1 = 0.05$ rad, $\dot{e}_1 = -0.1$ rad/s, $e_2 = 0.02$ rad, $\dot{e}_2 = 0.05$ rad/s):**
$$\tau_{fb,1} = 50(0.05) + 10(-0.1) = 2.5 - 1.0 = 1.5\ \text{N·m}$$
$$\tau_{fb,2} = 50(0.02) + 10(0.05) = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{N·m}$$

**Total DFC torques:**
$$\tau_1 = 2.177 + 1.5 = 3.677\ \text{N·m}, \quad \tau_2 = 0.766 + 1.5 = 2.266\ \text{N·m}.$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Control Architectures

| Property | Pure Decentralized | DFC (this topic) | Centralized Computed-Torque |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information flow** | Local only | Local + cross-FF signals | Global |
| **On-line FLOPs** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ (banded) | $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ |
| **Need full $M(q)$?** | No | Diagonal + cross-terms | Yes |
| **Stability proof** | Conditional (dominant diagonal) | Robust (Lyapunov) | Global (linearisation) |
| **Tracking accuracy** | Low–Medium | High | Highest |
| **Fault tolerance** | Excellent (per-joint) | Good (FF is auxiliary) | Poor (single-point) |
| **Industrial adoption** | Legacy CNC | **Current standard** | Research / high-end |
| **Sampling rate (typ.)** | 4 kHz | 1–4 kHz | $\le 1$ kHz |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Decentralized Feedforward Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refA[qd_i desired trajectory] --> sumJ1((+))
    refB[qd_j for j not i] --> sumJ1
    sumJ1 --> dynModel[Local Dynamic Model: M_ii v_i + sum M_ij v_j + sum C_ij qdot_j + G_i]
    dynModel --> ffOut[tau_ff_i DECENTRALIZED]
    ffOut --> totalSum((+))
    fbOut[tau_fb_i LOCAL PID] --> totalSum
    errSum[Error e_i = qd_i - q_i] --> pidBlock[Local PID: K_P K_D K_I]
    pidBlock --> fbOut
    totalSum --> actuator[i-th Joint Actuator]
    actuator --> jointDyn[Plant: M_ii qddot_i + coupling]
    jointDyn --> jointOut[Actual q_i]
    jointOut --> errSum
    jointOut --> crossCoupling[Cross-coupling to other joints: M_ij, C_ij]
    crossCoupling -.-> dynModel
```

```mermaid
        ffComp4[FF Joint 4: M44 v4 + M4j vj + C4j qdot_j + G4]
        ffComp5[FF Joint 5: M55 v5 + M5j vj + C5j qdot_j + G5]
        ffComp6[FF Joint 6: M66 v6 + M6j vj + C6j qdot_j + G6]
    end
    subgraph FB_LAYER [Local Feedback Layer - 4 kHz]
        fb1[Local PID Joint 1]
        fb2[Local PID Joint 2]
        fb3[Local PID Joint 3]
        fb4[Local PID Joint 4]
        fb5[Local PID Joint 5]
        fb6[Local PID Joint 6]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATORS [Joint Actuators - Hardware]

    end
    trajGen --> ffComp1

    trajGen --> ffComp6
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Increasing Feedforward Accuracy] --> B[Tracking Error Norm decreases]
    B --> C[Residual bounded by: norm M inverse times norm model uncertainty divided by lambda min K_P]
    A --> D[Computational Load increases modestly from O n to O n squared]
    B --> E[Stability region in K_P K_D plane EXPANDS]
```

### 4.4 Failure-Mode Flowchart — When DFC Breaks Down

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([DFC controller active]) --> check1{Model parameters up to date?}
    check1 -- NO --> fail1[Steady-state error grows: bounded by uncertainty term]
    check1 -- YES --> check2{Trajectory within row-dominance region?}
    check2 -- NO --> fail2[Stability lost at high speed - reduce gains]
    check2 -- YES --> check3{Actuator saturation?}
    check3 -- YES --> fail3[FF term saturates first - clip FF before FB]
    check3 -- NO --> ok[Stable tracking achieved]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *Decentralized Feedforward Compensation* in the context of robotic manipulator control. List any **two** advantages it offers over purely centralized computed-torque control.

**Model Answer:**
Decentralized Feedforward Compensation is a control architecture in which each joint of a manipulator is regulated by an **independent local feedback loop** (typically a PID), while the **off-diagonal coupling torques** of the manipulator's dynamic model are pre-computed and added as a *feedforward* signal at each joint.

*Two advantages:*
1. **Computational reduction:** the on-line matrix inversion of the full $n \times n$ inertia matrix $M(q)$ is replaced by $n$ scalar operations (diagonal elements), reducing the on-line complexity from $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
2. **Fault tolerance and modularity:** failure of the feedforward estimator in one joint does not destabilise the entire arm, since each joint has a robust local feedback loop. This is critical for safety-critical and space applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the role of the *diagonal dominance* property of the inertia matrix $M(q)$ in enabling decentralized manipulator control? Mention the Gershgorin-based condition briefly.

**Model Answer:**
When the inertia matrix $M(q)$ is diagonally dominant, the *local* dynamics at each joint dominate the *cross-coupling* dynamics between joints. This means a *local* (per-joint) controller can stabilise the joint in isolation, with the cross-coupling treated as a bounded disturbance. The Gershgorin-based sufficient condition for row-sum dominance is

$$|M_{ii}(q)| \;\ge\; \sum_{j \ne i} |M_{ij}(q)| \quad \text{for all } i, \; \text{for all } q \in \text{workspace}.$$

When this holds, the eigenvalues of $M(q)$ are clustered near the diagonal entries, so diagonal-only feedback is *conditionally stable*; the feedforward term then *augments* performance rather than *guaranteeing* stability.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the full Lagrangian dynamic model of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau,$$

**derive step-by-step** the decentralized feedforward compensation control law

$$\tau_i = M_{ii}(q)\,v_i + \sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij}(q)\,v_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_j + G_i(q) + K_{P,i}e_i + K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i,$$

clearly identifying the feedforward and feedback components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using a Lyapunov candidate $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_P e$, **prove** that the DFC closed-loop system is uniformly stable, and obtain a **bound on the steady-state tracking error** in the presence of bounded model uncertainty $\tilde{M}, \tilde{C}, \tilde{G}$.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the dynamic equation for joint $i$ by expanding the matrix-vector product row-wise:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_j + G_i(q) = \tau_i.$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify the centralized computed-torque objective. We desire a linear error dynamics. Define the auxiliary variable

$$v = \ddot{q}_d + K_D\dot{e} + K_P e,$$

and the centralised torque $\tau = M v + C \dot{q} + G$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Decompose $M v$ into diagonal and off-diagonal parts:

$$\tau_i = M_{ii} v_i + \sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij} v_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij} \dot{q}_j + G_i.$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Recognise that $v_i$ *itself* contains a feedback term $K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{P,i}e_i$ added to $\ddot{q}_{d,i}$. Hence $M_{ii} v_i = M_{ii}\ddot{q}_{d,i} + M_{ii}K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i + M_{ii}K_{P,i}e_i$. The last two are local-feedback contributions already in the feedforward term.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** To make the *feedback* purely local, add an additional explicit PD correction:

$$\tau_{fb,i} = K_{P,i}e_i + K_{D,i}\dot{e}_i.$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Combine to form the DFC law. The first four groups form the *decentralized feedforward*; the last two form the *local feedback*:

$$\tau_i = \underbrace{M_{ii} v_i + \sum_{j\ne i} M_{ij} v_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n} C_{ij} \dot{q}_j + G_i}_{\tau_{ff,i}^{dec}} + \underbrace{K_{P,i} e_i + K_{D,i} \dot{e}_i}_{\tau_{fb,i}}.$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Comment on the structure: the FF term is computable from the *known* model and reference trajectory; the FB term uses only the *locally measured* $q_i$ and $\dot{q}_i$. This makes the architecture decentralised despite including the cross-coupling terms in the FF.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Lyapunov candidate:

$$V(e, \dot e) = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_P e.$$

This is positive definite since $M(q) \succ 0$ (physical property of the inertia matrix) and $K_P \succ 0$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate $V$ with respect to time:

$$\dot V = \dot{e}^T M \ddot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot M \dot{e} + \dot{e}^T K_P e.$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute the closed-loop error dynamics in the *nominal* (model-perfect) case. From the centralised computed-torque derivation, $M\ddot{e} + C\dot{e} + K_D\dot{e} + K_P e = 0$, so $M\ddot{e} = -C\dot{e} - K_D \dot{e} - K_P e$. Substituting:

$$\dot V = \dot{e}^T(-C\dot e - K_D \dot e - K_P e) + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot M \dot e + \dot{e}^T K_P e = -\dot{e}^T K_D \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T(\dot M - 2C)\dot e.$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Invoke the **passivity / skew-symmetry property** $\dot M - 2C = -(\dot M - 2C)^T$, which gives $\dot{e}^T(\dot M - 2C)\dot e = 0$. Therefore

$$\dot V = -\dot e^T K_D \dot e \le 0.$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude uniform stability. Since $\dot V \le 0$, $V$ is non-increasing, so $V(t) \le V(0)$ for all $t \ge 0$. Boundedness of $V$ implies boundedness of $e$ and $\dot e$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Steady-state error bound in the presence of model uncertainty. With bounded uncertainty $\eta(t) = M^{-1}(\tilde M \ddot q + \tilde C \dot q + \tilde G)$ and $\|\eta\| \le \eta_{\max}$, the error dynamics become

$$\ddot e + M^{-1}(C+K_D)\dot e + M^{-1}K_P e = \eta(t).$$

In steady state, $\dot e \to 0$ and $\ddot e \to 0$, leaving $M^{-1}K_P e_{ss} = \eta_{ss}$. Therefore

$$\|e_{ss}\| \;\le\; \frac{\eta_{\max}}{\lambda_{\min}(M^{-1} K_P)} = \frac{\eta_{\max} \cdot \lambda_{\max}(M)}{\lambda_{\min}(K_P)}.$$

This completes the Lyapunov proof and the error bound.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **clearly labelled block diagram**, explain the architecture of a Decentralized Feedforward Compensation system for a 2-DOF manipulator. Identify the central planner, the FF-compensation layer, the local feedback layer, the actuator layer, and the cross-coupling signals.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2-DOF planar arm has

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 3.776 & 1.103 \\ 1.103 & 0.53 \end{bmatrix}\ \text{kg·m}^2, \quad C(q,\dot q)\dot q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0177 & -0.0177 \\ 0.0355 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix}.$$

For $q_d = [0.5,\, 0.3]^T$ rad, $\ddot q_d = [0.4,\, 0.6]^T$ rad/s², $K_{P,i} = 50$, $K_{D,i} = 10$, $e = [0.05,\, 0.02]^T$ rad, $\dot e = [-0.1,\, 0.05]^T$ rad/s, compute the **DFC torques $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$**.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block diagram description (4 marks):**

| # | Block | Function | Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Central Trajectory Planner | Generates $q_d(t), \dot q_d(t), \ddot q_d(t)$ | 100 Hz |
| 2 | Decentralized FF Layer | Computes $\tau_{ff,i}^{dec} = M_{ii}v_i + \sum_{j \ne i}M_{ij}v_j + \sum_j C_{ij}\dot q_j + G_i$ | 1 kHz |
| 3 | Local Feedback Layer (PID) | Computes $\tau_{fb,i} = K_P e_i + K_D \dot e_i + K_I \int e_i$ | 4 kHz |
| 4 | Torque Summation Node | $\tau_i = \tau_{ff,i}^{dec} + \tau_{fb,i}$ | 4 kHz |
| 5 | Actuator (Servo Drive) | Applies $\tau_i$ to joint motor | Hardware |
| 6 | Plant (Joint) | Produces $q_i, \dot q_i$ | Continuous |
| 7 | Cross-Coupling Bus | Distributes $\dot q_j, \ddot q_j$ ($j \ne i$) to FF layer of joint $i$ | 1 kHz |

**Three key observations to mention (3 marks):**
1. The local feedback runs at 4 kHz — *faster* than the FF layer (1 kHz), giving the architecture intrinsic robustness to FF computation delays.
2. The cross-coupling bus is *one-way* (from joints to FF layer) — there is *no* joint-to-joint feedback, preserving decentralisation.
3. The central planner is *not* in the fast loop — its role is purely trajectory generation, so its failure cannot destabilise the arm in real time.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the auxiliary variable $v_i = \ddot q_{d,i} + K_{D,i}\dot e_i + K_{P,i}e_i$:
- $v_1 = 0.4 + 10(-0.1) + 50(0.05) = 0.4 - 1.0 + 2.5 = 1.9$ rad/s²
- $v_2 = 0.6 + 10(0.05) + 50(0.02) = 0.6 + 0.5 + 1.0 = 2.1$ rad/s²

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the Coriolis term $C \dot q$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 0.0177(0.2) + (-0.0177)(-0.1) \\ 0.0355(0.2) + 0(-0.1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00354 + 0.00177 \\ 0.00710 + 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00531 \\ 0.00710 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Diagonal inertia terms: $M_{11} = 3.776$, $M_{22} = 0.53$. Off-diagonal: $M_{12} = M_{21} = 1.103$. Gravity $G = 0$.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute feedforward torque for joint 1:
$$\tau_{ff,1}^{dec} = M_{11}v_1 + M_{12}v_2 + (C\dot q)_1 + G_1$$
$$= 3.776(1.9) + 1.103(2.1) + 0.00531 + 0 = 7.174 + 2.316 + 0.005 = 9.495\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute feedforward torque for joint 2:
$$\tau_{ff,2}^{dec} = M_{22}v_2 + M_{21}v_1 + (C\dot q)_2 + G_2$$
$$= 0.53(2.1) + 1.103(1.9) + 0.00710 + 0 = 1.113 + 2.096 + 0.007 = 3.216\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Add local PD feedback:
$$\tau_{fb,1} = K_{P,1}e_1 + K_{D,1}\dot e_1 = 50(0.05) + 10(-0.1) = 2.5 - 1.0 = 1.5\ \text{N·m}$$
$$\tau_{fb,2} = K_{P,2}e_2 + K_{D,2}\dot e_2 = 50(0.02) + 10(0.05) = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{N·m}$$

**Step 7 (Bonus / final step):** Total DFC torques:
$$\tau_1 = 9.495 + 1.5 = 10.995 \approx 11.0\ \text{N·m}$$
$$\tau_2 = 3.216 + 1.5 = 4.716 \approx 4.72\ \text{N·m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign of the Coriolis term:** Many students compute $C_{ij}$ correctly but then forget to multiply by $\dot q_j$ — the *full* Coriolis *vector* is $C(q,\dot q)\dot q$, not $C(q,\dot q)$ alone. **Lose 1 mark** if $C_{ij}\dot q_j$ is missing.
> 2. **Forgetting the diagonal inertia cross-term:** When the off-diagonal $M_{12}$ is *not* negligible (e.g., when $l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$ is comparable to $l_1^2$), students often drop the $\sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij} v_j$ term. **Lose 2 marks** — this is precisely the "decentralized feedforward" the question is testing.
> 3. **Confusing $v_i$ with $\ddot q_i$:** $v_i$ is the *reference* acceleration *plus* the stabilising feedback term $\ddot q_{d,i} + K_{D,i}\dot e_i + K_{P,i} e_i$. If you substitute the *measured* $\ddot q_i$ instead, the feedforward becomes a *feedback*, breaking decentralisation. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 4. **Units in the final answer:** Always quote $\tau$ in **N·m** for revolute joints and **N** for prismatic. The examiner deducts 0.5 mark for missing units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DFC = Independent Joint PID + Model-Based Cross-Coupling Feedforward.** It is *not* centralised (no full $M(q)$ inversion) and *not* purely decentralised (cross-coupling *is* compensated).
- The **decoupling identity** $M(q)\ddot q = M_{ii}\ddot q_i + \sum_{j \ne i} M_{ij}\ddot q_j$ is the *single most important equation* in this topic.
- The control law has **two parts**: $\tau = \tau_{ff}^{dec} + \tau_{fb}$, where $\tau_{ff}^{dec}$ uses the *reference* trajectory and $\tau_{fb}$ uses the *measured* error.
- The **stability proof** relies on the passivity property $\dot M - 2C$ being skew-symmetric, giving $\dot V = -\dot e^T K_D \dot e \le 0$.
- The **steady-state error bound** is $\|e_{ss}\| \le \lambda_{\max}(M)\eta_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}(K_P)$, where $\eta_{\max}$ is the model-uncertainty bound.
- **Computational savings:** $\mathcal{O}(n^3) \to \mathcal{O}(n^2)$ (or $\mathcal{O}(n)$ if only the diagonal is used), the *commercial* reason industry uses DFC.
- **Sampling rates:** local feedback $\ge 1$ kHz; feedforward $\ge 250$ Hz; central planner $\le 100$ Hz. Mismatching these violates the architecture.
- **Diagonal dominance** of $M(q)$ is the *enabling condition* for decentralised control; without it, DFC is unstable regardless of gains.
- **Real-world use cases** to mention in any exam answer: KUKA KR series, ABB IRB series, Canadarm, Da Vinci surgical system.
- **The KTU 2024 marking scheme** typically gives: 3 marks for definition, 4 marks for derivation/architecture, 3 marks for computation/proof, 2 marks for industrial context, and 2 marks for neat diagrams and units.

---
